USER GUIDE FOR MEDICAL SUMMARY
For maximum compatibility and best results, we recommend that you use Adobe Reader
software to read and navigate this document.
For the free Adobe Reader version, click the link: https:7//get.adobe.com/reader
The medical summary structure
>»Case Overview: Introduces the narrative of the case with a short human-like summary and provides a
chronological review of the information needed to analyze a case including a chronological timeline
and overview of the impairments, recent medical history such as most recent visit, medications,
procedures and abnormal lab result.
>Impairment Rundown: An impairment level focus of the case provides a 360° view for each of the
conditions found in the case including comorbidities, associated symptoms, medications, labs,
procedures and specialist visits.
>»Document Abstract: A chronologically ordered summary of each medical encounter available in the
source documents in two different formats: human-like summaries or text-to-text transcription for each
of the encounters.
>»Source Documents: A copy ofall the source documents uploaded for the case, with highlighting of key
data used in the Digital Medical Abstract.
>»Supplemental Documents: Contains additional medical information, including key health indicators,
conditions, procedures and medications references, lab results and a general index.
How to navigate the medical summary efficiently:
In Adobe Reader, look for the Bookmarks button A on the left side of the screen and click on it
(or alternatively: View > Show/Hide > Navigation Panes > Bookmarks). The bookmarks should be used as
a “Table of Contents" menu to enjoy maximum efficiency in reading the summary.
Bookmarks enable a “split-screen” experience where the medical data is always available when viewing
the source documents of the medical file.
This is a clickable PDF summary! All diagnosis, dates and medical data points are clickable. Click on
the data point and jump directly to the source page in the medical file from which it was extracted.

the previously-viewed page. Alternatively you can use the shortcut ALT+LEFT.

Case Overview
Summary Date: 06.19.2024
Case Name: Caryn McCay
Prepared For: B2C
Total Pages: 1176 (10.05.2010 - 06.19.2024)
Handwritten/Other Pages: 25
Name: Mccay, Caryn L.
DOB: 11.30.1972 (51 yo)
Sex: Female
Date Of Loss: 07.30.2023
The patient is a 51-year-old female with a history of injury, pain, and limited range of motion. She
was involved in a motor vehicle accident in which she suffered from lumbar, right anterior knee, and
left anterior knee pain. The pain is rated as 5 out of 10, with 10 being the worst. The complaint has
worsened since the onset and is radiating to her feet bilaterally. She also has a history of a posterior
neck pain that is rated as 5 out of 10, with 10 being the worst. The complaint has worsened since
the onset and is radiating to her feet bilaterally. She was diagnosed with cervical spondylolysis,
lumbosacral root disorders, spinal enthesopathy, myalgia of the head and neck muscles, posttraumatic stress disorder, and difficulty walking. She had a CT of the abdomen and pelvis due to left
upper quadrant pain, which showed moderate to large amount of stool throughout the colon,
suggesting constipation. In addition, there was a 1 cm nodule in the subcutaneous tissues of the
anterior right lower abdomen, which probably represents a sebaceous cyst.

Eric, Moghadamian - Orthopaedic Surgery (Orthopaedic Trauma)
The patient presents for a knee X-ray due to pain. The study shows mild to moderate medial and
indeterminate lateral femorotibial compartment osteoporosis, small suprapatellar joint effusion, and
moderate patellofemoral osteoarthrosis in the left knee. The right knee has healing tibia and fibular
fractures, as well as mild to moderate tricompartmental osteoarthrosis, and a small suprapatellar joint
effusion. The patient is diagnosed with degenerative changes with healing right tibia and fibular fractures.

Go To Abstract | Go To Source Document
07.30.2023
Glenn, Irby - Student in an Organized Health Care Education/Training Program
The patient presents to the emergency department from the scene for evaluation as a trauma due to a
motor vehicle crash. She was a restrained driver without ejection, and she reports driving at approximately
45mph when another car pulled out in front of her, causing her to hit a brick wall. The patient believes she
may have lost consciousness for a brief period during the accident. She is currently experiencing pain in
her chest, cervical, and thoracic spine without associated weakness, numbness, and tingling. She
underwent lab tests, which showed hyperglycemia with elevated beta hydroxybutyrate of 0.62, A1c of 13.5
%, and abnormal aPTT of 21. She underwent imaging that revealed no acute findings or traumatic injuries.
The patient is in stable condition and amenable to discharge at this time.
Go To Abstract | Go To Source Document
07.30.2023
Diana, Labrada - Emergency Medicine (CHANDLER)
The patient presents to the emergency department following a motor vehicle crash. She has a history of
elevated blood sugar level, and she is diagnosed with traumatic chest pain and a motor vehicle collision,
initial encounter. The patient was prescribed Hydrocodone, Oxycodone, Morphine, and Fentanyl.

and was referred to a pain specialist and a chiropractor. She has a history of hernia surgery in 2016, MVA in
2016 leading to multiple LE fractures, tumor in R knee diagnosed prior to MVA not thought to be
cancerous, diabethic neuropathy, rectal cancer, and DM II. The patient is having difficulty with going up and
down stairs, as well as difficulty with walking between rooms, getting into or out of the bath, squatting,
lifting an object like a bag of groceries from the floor, and performing heavy activities around her home.
The patient will continue her physical therapy treatment.

Knee disorder
Mentions between
03/13 - 03/24
(10 years prior to Date 
Of Loss)
Large Effusion In 
Suprapatellar Pouch 
Tear Of Meniscus 
Effusion Of Right Knee 
Joint 
Left Knee Effusion 
03.12.2024
Harris, Jason S
The patient presents for an MRI of the right knee with and without contrast. The study shows a complex
tear involving the medial meniscus, moderate chondromalacia within the medial compartment, mild-tomoderate chondromalacia within the lateral compartment with areas of full-thickness cartilage loss and a
suspected internal osteophyte formation, there is also an irregularity with fragmentation of the lateral
tibial plateau which is presumably a sequela of prior comminuted depressed tibial plateau fracture, there
is mild cortical irregularity along the articular surface of the lateral tibial plateau, there is a very low T1 and
T2 signal lesion within the lateral epiphysis and metaphysis of the lateral tibia, there is ganglion cyst
formation noted at the gastrocnemius muscle attachments, there are severe arthritic changes in the
patellofemoral compartment with complete loss of articular cartilage seen along the lateral facet with
extensive subchondral and/or osteochondral-type defects, there is osteophytosis, there is a small
suprapatellar joint effusion. The patient was referred to radiographic correlation.
Go To Abstract | Go To Source Document
06.22.2016
Eric, Moghadamian - Orthopaedic Surgery (Orthopaedic Trauma)
The patient presents for a knee X-ray due to pain. The study shows mild to moderate medial and
indeterminate lateral femorotibial compartment osteoporosis, small suprapatellar joint effusion, and
moderate patellofemoral osteoarthrosis in the left knee. The right knee has healing tibia and fibular
fractures, as well as mild to moderate tricompartmental osteoarthrosis, and a small suprapatellar joint
effusion. The patient is diagnosed with degenerative changes with healing right tibia and fibular fractures.
Go To Abstract | Go To Source Document
06.22.2016
Eric, Moghadamian - Orthopaedic Surgery (Orthopaedic Trauma)
The patient presents for a knee 3 views x-ray due to pain. The study shows left knee with mild to moderate
medial and indeterminate lateral femorotibial compartment osteoporosis, small suprapatellar joint
effusion, and moderate patellofemoral osteoarthrosis. The right knee has healing tibia and fibular

This document contains private information – any unlawful disclosure is prohibited
fractures, mild to moderate tricompartmental osteoarthrosis, and a small suprapatellar joint effusion. The

Lumbar Spine 
Movement Pain 
Lumbar Discomfort 
Pain In Cervical Spine 
04.16.2024
Brock, H Marrs - Student in an Organized Health Care Education/Training Program
The patient presents for a follow-up evaluation of her diabetic neuropathy. She has been smoking
cigarettes for 37.00 years and has a history of car accident in 2016. She was on Lyrica for neuropathic pain
from her spine, but she switched to Gabapentin and experienced a sedative component. The patient
reports that she fell within the last week and was given Tramadol and Flexeril for back pain, which she is
almost out of. She has bilateral diabetic foot neuropathy that has worsened over the past year affecting
her bilateral feet. The patient will increase her Pregabalin to 75 mg BID and return to the clinic in 1 month
to reassess response to the changed medications.
Go To Abstract | Go To Source Document
04.16.2024
Brock, H Marrs - Student in an Organized Health Care Education/Training Program
The patient presents for a follow-up evaluation of her diabetic neuropathy. She has been smoking
cigarettes for 37.00 years and has a history of car accident in 2016. She was on Lyrica for neuropathic pain
from her spine, but she had to stop due to the sedative component. The patient reports that she fell within
the last week and was given Tramadol and Flexeril for which she is almost out, but reports that they have
helped her back pain. She has a history of poor healing gluteal, foot wounds. The patient will continue with
medication therapy and will return to the clinic in 1 month to reassess response to the change in
medications.

The patient presents for neuropathic pain. She has a history of smoking cigarettes and has been exposed to
tobacco smoke. She was on Lyrica for neuropathic pain from her spine, but she is currently taking
Gabapentin. She fell within the last week and was given Tramadol and Flexeril for her back pain. The
patient will continue Gabapentin and will also start taking Lyrica. She will return to the clinic in 1 month to
reassess her response to the change in medications.

Spinal fractures
Mentions between
02/11 - 08/23
(12 years prior to Date 
Of Loss)
Closed Fracture Of 
Coccyx 
08.18.2023
Mit, Patel
The patient presents to the ED with concerns of a left peritonsillar abscess. She reports symptoms of left
throat and neck pain along with odynophagia that began 2 days ago. She has a history of diabetes, anxiety
disorder, fracture of coccyx, and endocrine, nutritional and metabolic disease. The patient underwent
flexible fiberoptic laryngoscopy which revealed an area of polypoid change anteriorly on the right vocal
fold, but not concerning in appearance. The patient was diagnosed with a small right sided abscess that
was lateral and extended inferiorly to the epiglottis. The abscess was drained at the bedside with purulent
drainage and she reported near immediate improvement in her symptoms. The patient was prescribed
Augmentin for 7-10 days and referred for follow-up in 3-4 weeks.

Go To Abstract | Go To Source Document
11.21.2023
Leverette, Nicholas
The patient presents for a therapeutic exercises appointment. She is here to continue her physical therapy
and was referred to a pain specialist and a chiropractor. She has a history of hernia surgery in 2016, MVA in
2016 leading to multiple LE fractures, tumor in R knee diagnosed prior to MVA not thought to be
cancerous, diabethic neuropathy, rectal cancer, and DM II. The patient is having difficulty with going up and
down stairs, as well as difficulty with walking between rooms, getting into or out of the bath, squatting,
lifting an object like a bag of groceries from the floor, and performing heavy activities around her home.
The patient will continue her physical therapy treatment.

narrowing and osteophytosis. There is deformity of the proximal fibula likely related to prior fracture.

Relevant Therapeutic Reports
In the 24 months prior to 06.19.2024
Date Type Abstract
12.12.2023 Follow-Up on Multiple Orthopedics 
Conditions
Leverette, Nicholas
The patient presents for a chiropractic therapy appointment. The patient has a
history of MVA on 7/30/23. She was diagnosed with cervical quad syndrome,
lumbosacral disorders, right trapezius pain, left trapezius pain, right levator scapula
pain, left levator scapula pain, right sternocleidomastoid pain, left
sternocleidomastoid pain, right longus capitis pain, left longus capitis pain, right
lumbar spine enthesopathy, left lumbar spine enthesopathy, right cervical spine
spondylolysis, and left cervical spine spondylolysis. She is referred to physical
therapy.
Go To Source Document
12.12.2023 Evaluation for Physical Therapy
Leverette, Nicholas
The patient presents to skilled physical therapy for evaluation with primary concern
of bilateral ankle and knee pain which have been present since she was in an MVA
on 7/30/23. She was t-boned and her knees hit the dash forcefully. She reports her
pain is worse R > L, and rates her current pain a 6/10, pain at worst 10/10 in last 1-2
weeks, and pain at best 5/10. She has difficulty getting up / down from the floor as
well. The patient is working 2 days a week at the VA after 2 months off work and
plans to start full - time in 4 weeks. She reports she has to lay down when she gets
home from work and sometimes while on breaks at work due to pain. Her goal with
physical therapy is to reduce symptoms in order to return to prior level of function
and work full - time at the VA.
Go To Source Document

Date Type Abstract
12.11.2023 Chiropractic Therapy Visit
Brown, Sara
The patient presents for a therapeutic visit. The patient's diagnosis is pain in the right
ankle, sacroiliac joint, left knee, and hip. The patient was treated with manual
therapy and exercises. The patient was instructed to perform isometric exercises to
the affected body region.
Go To Source Document
12.11.2023 Cervicobrachial syndrome 
diagnosis and treatment plan
Brown, Sara
The patient presents for a chiropractic therapy visit. She has been experiencing
discomfort in the neck, which she describes as tightness and stiffness. The patient is
able to perform her job duties with some difficulty. The following activities are
limited due to the condition: lifting, bending, standing, walking, sitting, sleeping,
driving, and exercising. The patient has been diagnosed with segmental somatic
dysfunction of the cervical spine, spondylolysis of the cervical spine region, and
multiple spinal enthesopathy at various sites in the spine. She will increase her
physical therapy exercises and will follow up in 8 weeks for a re-evaluation.
Go To Source Document
12.07.2023 Chiropractic Therapy Visit
Brown, Sara
The patient presents for a chiropractic therapy appointment. The patient is
experiencing pain in the right elbow, left elbow, right knee, left knee, right ankle, and
left ankle. The patient is diagnosed with lumbar somatic dysfunction, chronic posttraumatic stress disorder, and pain in the right ankle. The patient is treated with
manual therapy exercises and neuromuscular re-education.
Go To Source Document
12.05.2023 Chiropractic Therapy Visit
Brown, Sara
The patient presents for a chiropractic therapy appointment. The patient is
diagnosed with pain in the right ankle, knee, and left foot. The patient was treated
with hot packs on the knees, manual therapy, and therapeutic exercises.
Go To Source Document
12.05.2023 Caryn Chiropractic Therapy Visit
Brown, Sara
The patient presents for a chiropractic therapy appointment. The patient has a
diagnosis of segmental and somatic dysfunction of the cervical spine, spondylolysis
of the cervical region, injury of nerve root of cervical spine, and muscle spasms of
the back. The patient will increase their LEFS score by 15 points in order to
demonstrate a significant increase in level of function and tolerance to functional
tasks.
Go To Source Document

Brown, Sara

right ankle. The patient was treated with manual therapy and exercises.

Skin infection
Mentioned on
08/23
(19 days after Date Of 
Loss)
Phlegmon (suspected) 08.18.2023
Brennan, Noel S - Student in an Organized Health Care Education/Training Program
The patient presents with an abscess. She was transferred from the outside hospital with complaints of 2
days of dysphagia, odynophagia, and left-sided neck pain. The patient had a CT neck with contrast which
demonstrated an underlying 1.8 cm focus of developing phlegmon/abscess that displaced the airway to
the right and there was mild to moderate narrowing of the airway. The patient had a peritonsillar abscess
and was seen and examined by Dr. Bowers. The abscess was drained at bedside by ENT and she is doing
well now.

Mentions between
01/16 - 01/24
(7 years prior to Date Of 
Loss)
Recurrent Incisional 
Hernia 
Umbilical Hernia 
01.11.2024
George, Munday S (EPHRAIM MCDOWELL SURGEONS)
The patient presents with a recurrent hernia after a car accident. She underwent a robotic assisted
laparoscopic repair of an incarcerated ventral hernia with a mesh back in February 2018. The patient has
been having pain in the site of her hernia repair and has had a CT scan since the accident, but not since the
time of the crash. She was diagnosed with abdominal pain in the left upper quadrant and was referred for
a CT of the abdomen and pelvis without contrast to rule out a hernia recurrence.

follow-up.

08.18.2023
J, Jennings Nathan
The patient presents to the ED with a 2 day history of neck and ear pain. The patient has a history of
hypertension and diabetes. A CTA showed a peritonsillar abscess. The patient was given Toradol,

Go To Abstract | Go To Source Document
08.18.2023
Joshua, Sykes - Physician Assistant
The patient presents with throat pain. She is diagnosed with a peritonsillar abscess. The patient underwent
a CT scan, which showed an asymmetric enlargement of the left palatine tonsillar area with an underlying
1.8 cm focus of developing phlegmon/abscess. This displaced the airway to the right and there was mild to
moderate narrowing of the airway. There was edema and probable fluid that extended inferiorly in the
posterior pharynx, effacing the left piriform sinus. The patient is strep negative, and her labs are today are
negative for diabetes mellitus with a glucose level of 325. The patient is prescribed 1 gram of Vancomycin
and 600 mg of Clindamycin.

Brock, H Marrs, MD

The patient presents for a follow-up evaluation of her diabetic neuropathy. She has
been smoking cigarettes for 37.00 years and has a history of car accident in 2016.
She was on Lyrica for neuropathic pain from her spine, but she had to stop due to
the sedative component. The patient reports that she fell within the last week and
was given Tramadol and Flexeril for which she is almost out, but reports that they

This document contains private information – any unlawful disclosure is prohibited
have helped her back pain. She has a history of poor healing gluteal, foot wounds.

month to reassess response to the change in medications.

Follow Up for Diabetic Neuropathy
The patient presents for a follow-up evaluation of her diabetic neuropathy. She has
been smoking cigarettes for 37.00 years and has a history of car accident in 2016.
She was on Lyrica for neuropathic pain from her spine, but she switched to
Gabapentin and experienced a sedative component. The patient reports that she fell
within the last week and was given Tramadol and Flexeril for back pain, which she is
almost out of. She has bilateral diabetic foot neuropathy that has worsened over the
past year affecting her bilateral feet. The patient will increase her Pregabalin to 75
mg BID and return to the clinic in 1 month to reassess response to the changed
medications.

03.19.2024 Encounter for Neuropathic Pain
Harden, R William, DO
Student in an Organized Health Care 
Education/Training Program
The patient presents for neuropathic pain. She has a history of smoking cigarettes
and has been exposed to tobacco smoke. She was on Lyrica for neuropathic pain
from her spine following a car wreck in 2016, and she reports that it helped her. She
fell within the last week and was given Tramadol and Flexeril for her back pain, which
she is almost out of. She has a diagnosis of chronic worsening neuropathy,
uncontrolled diabetes mellitus type 2, severe facet arthropathy, and hypertrophy in
the lumbar spine. The patient was prescribed Gabapentin for neuropathic pain but
has been struggling with the sedation. Therefore, they switched to Lyrica today. She
will return to the clinic in 1 month to reassess her response to the change in
medications.

Gabapentin and will also start taking Lyrica. She will return to the clinic in 1 month to
reassess her response to the change in medications.

03.12.2024 
11:50:00
MRI of the Right Knee with and 
without Contrast
Harris, Jason S, M.D .
The patient presents for an MRI of the right knee with and without contrast. The
study shows a complex tear involving the medial meniscus, moderate
chondromalacia within the medial compartment, mild-to-moderate chondromalacia
within the lateral compartment with areas of full-thickness cartilage loss and a
suspected internal osteophyte formation, there is also an irregularity with
fragmentation of the lateral tibial plateau which is presumably a sequela of prior
comminuted depressed tibial plateau fracture, there is mild cortical irregularity along
the articular surface of the lateral tibial plateau, there is a very low T1 and T2 signal
lesion within the lateral epiphysis and metaphysis of the lateral tibia, there is
ganglion cyst formation noted at the gastrocnemius muscle attachments, there are
severe arthritic changes in the patellofemoral compartment with complete loss of
articular cartilage seen along the lateral facet with extensive subchondral and/or
osteochondral-type defects, there is osteophytosis, there is a small suprapatellar
joint effusion. The patient was referred to radiographic correlation.

16:52:00
Caryn Leigh Mccay's Multiple 
Abnormal Uptake of the Knees
Harris, Jason S, M.D .
The patient underwent a nuclear medicine limited bone scan due to right knee pain.
The study shows small field of view imaging of the knees with bilateral uptake. The
left knee has a focal area of uptake along the medial tibial plateau and a larger area
along the lateral femoral condyle. The right knee has more diffuse uptake seen about
the knee articulations both centrally along the peripheries. There is also diffuse
involvement of the patella. The study shows multifocal abnormal uptake about the
knees bilaterally, with worse right sided asymmetry.

12.12.2023 Evaluation for Physical Therapy
Leverette, Nicholas
The patient presents to skilled physical therapy for evaluation with primary concern
of bilateral ankle and knee pain which have been present since she was in an MVA
on 7/30/23. She was t-boned and her knees hit the dash forcefully. She reports her
pain is worse R > L, and rates her current pain a 6/10, pain at worst 10/10 in last 1-2
weeks, and pain at best 5/10. She has difficulty getting up / down from the floor as
well. The patient is working 2 days a week at the VA after 2 months off work and
plans to start full - time in 4 weeks. She reports she has to lay down when she gets
home from work and sometimes while on breaks at work due to pain. Her goal with

The patient presents with an abscess. She was transferred from the outside hospital
with complaints of 2 days of dysphagia, odynophagia, and left-sided neck pain. The
patient had a CT neck with contrast which demonstrated an underlying 1.8 cm focus
of developing phlegmon/abscess that displaced the airway to the right and there
was mild to moderate narrowing of the airway. The patient had a peritonsillar
abscess and was seen and examined by Dr. Bowers. The abscess was drained at
bedside by ENT and she is doing well now.
Go To Source Document

This document contains private information – any unlawful disclosure is prohibited
08.18.2023 18:06:00 - Abscess Evaluation and Care
The patient was evaluated in the emergency department for an abscess. The patient
is instructed to keep the areas surrounding the abscess clean and dry. The patient is
also prescribed antibiotics and advised to follow up with their primary care physician
within 2 days for a wound check.
Go To Source Document
08.18.2023 - Emergency Department Visit for Small ParapharyngealAbscess on Left
The patient presents to the ED with concerns of a left peritonsillar abscess. She
reports symptoms of left throat and neck pain along with odynophagia that began 2
days ago. She has a history of diabetes, anxiety disorder, fracture of coccyx, and
endocrine, nutritional and metabolic disease. The patient underwent flexible
fiberoptic laryngoscopy which revealed an area of polypoid change anteriorly on the
right vocal fold, but not concerning in appearance. The patient was diagnosed with a
small right sided abscess that was lateral and extended inferiorly to the epiglottis.
The abscess was drained at the bedside with purulent drainage and she reported
near immediate improvement in her symptoms. The patient was prescribed
Augmentin for 7-10 days and referred for follow-up in 3-4 weeks.
Go To Source Document
08.19.2023 16:47:00 - Emergency Department Facility Charge Scores
The patient is referred to an ENT/otolaryngology consultation.
Go To Source Document
08.18.2023 Emergency Department Visit due 
to Throat Pain
Joshua, Sykes, PA
Physician Assistant
The patient presents with throat pain. She is diagnosed with a peritonsillar abscess.
The patient underwent a CT scan, which showed an asymmetric enlargement of the
left palatine tonsillar area with an underlying 1.8 cm focus of developing phlegmon/
abscess. This displaced the airway to the right and there was mild to moderate
narrowing of the airway. There was edema and probable fluid that extended
inferiorly in the posterior pharynx, effacing the left piriform sinus. The patient is
strep negative, and her labs are today are negative for diabetes mellitus with a
glucose level of 325. The patient is prescribed 1 gram of Vancomycin and 600 mg of
Clindamycin.
Go To Source Document

07.30.2023 22:26:00 - Evaluation for Motor Vehicle Crash
The patient presents to the emergency department from the scene for evaluation as
a trauma due to a motor vehicle crash. She was a restrained driver without ejection,
and she reports driving at approximately 45mph when another car pulled out in
front of her, causing her to hit a brick wall. The patient believes she may have lost
consciousness for a brief period during the accident. She is currently experiencing
pain in her chest, cervical, and thoracic spine without associated weakness,
numbness, and tingling. She underwent lab tests, which showed hyperglycemia with
elevated beta hydroxybutyrate of 0.62, A1c of 13.5 %, and abnormal aPTT of 21. She
underwent imaging that revealed no acute findings or traumatic injuries. The patient
is in stable condition and amenable to discharge at this time.
Go To Source Document

right to left and entirely in cassettes A1-A2 to include the ends in A1.

Medical Evaluation
Boyd, Lan S, MD
The patient presents for a medical evaluation. She was arrested for shoplifting while
high on heroin and crack, and the police believed that she was attempting to hide
drugs by concealing them inside of her body, so she was brought here for an
evaluation. She underwent an abdomen x-ray, vaginal and rectal searches, and lab
tests, which showed elevated cocaine and fentanyl levels from the original day. Also,
today they found her cocaine and fentanyl levels to be elevated from that original
day, so they sent her here to have a CT of her abdomen. The patient underwent a CT
of the abdomen which did not show any foreign body or material.

Left Knee 3-View X-ray Report
Eric, Moghadamian, MD
Orthopaedic Surgery (Orthopaedic 
Trauma)
The patient presents for a knee 3 views left.
Go To Source Document
Left and Right Knee X-ray due to fracture of patella
The patient presents for a knee X-ray due to pain. The study shows mild to moderate
medial and indeterminate lateral femorotibial compartment osteoporosis, small
suprapatellar joint effusion, and moderate patellofemoral osteoarthrosis in the left
knee. The right knee has healing tibia and fibular fractures, as well as mild to
moderate tricompartmental osteoarthrosis, and a small suprapatellar joint effusion.
The patient is diagnosed with degenerative changes with healing right tibia and
fibular fractures.
Go To Source Document
05.13.2016 
10:25:00
CT of Right Patella
D, Raymond Wright, MD
The patient presents for an X-ray of the right knee due to pain. The study shows a
mild femorotibial compartment osteoarthrosis with soft tissue swelling, moderate
patellofemoral osteoarthrosis, and moderate suprapatellar joint effusion. The
patient's tibia and fibular fractures are also seen to be healing. In addition, the study
shows calcaneal enthesophytes and mild tricompartmental osteoarthrosis. In
summary, the patient has a closed fracture of the unspecified patella. In addition,
the study showed degenerative changes in the left knee without acute abnormality.
Go To Source Document
03.05.2014 - Billicit Patellofemoral Compartmental Osteoarthrosis
The patient presents for a knee X-ray due to pain in the knee. The study shows mild
medial femorotibial compartmental narrowing is noted bilaterally, moderate
patellofemoral compartmental KSM narrowing with moderate suprapatellar joint
effusion, and a fabella is present. The patient has osteoarthrosis, and the greatest is
in the patellofemoral compartment.
Go To Source Document

04.17.2016 Follow Up for Right Tibial plateau 
fracture
Dustin, H Hamilton, MD
The patient presents for an orthopaedic compartment check. The patient is a 43-
year-old female with a right tibial plateau fracture and a fracture of the coccyx. The
patient's pain is controlled, and there were no acute events. The patient's sensation
is intact to light touch superficial peroneal, saphenous, and sural nerve distributions.
The patient has paresthesias in the deep peroneal and tibial nerves unchanged since
last exam. The patient's AROM was 5/5 in the right lower extremity with no pain
with passive stretch. Additionally, the patient had mild distress, alert and oriented
x3, regular rate and rhythm, non-labored breathing, symmetric chest wall excursion,
non-tender abdomen, and well perfused toes with a capillary refill less than 2
seconds.
Go To Source Document

Evaluation
Eric, Moghadamian, MD
Orthopaedic Surgery (Orthopaedic 
Trauma)
The patient presents with a closed, acute, traumatic left patella fracture, and a
comminuted fracture of the coccyx. She was involved in a motor vehicle collision and
was a front passenger in a vehicle that was struck by another car. The patient
complains of rear end pain, right leg pain, and left patella pain. She was prescribed a
donut for comfort for the coccyx fracture and will follow up with an orthopedic
surgery trauma evaluation.

minimally displaced fracture of the fibular head. There is moderate severity
tricompartmental osteoarthritis and associated soft tissue swelling.

Narrative
ECI1 jispatched for vehicle collision at above loc CTT scene. E21 g dasses:
sitting in drivers seat and alert to crew arrival. Patient complaint of left arm pain, left |pain, neck pain and head pain. Patient advised she was traveling
about 45 mph and was unrestrained. Air bag deployed. Spidering on front windshield was noted by crew. Patient was placedinC collar. Crew used long
board to moved patient from vehicle. Patient denied loss of consciousness. Patient was fully immobilized by crew. Patient was secured to stretcher and
moved toambulance without incident. Vitals taken, IV established, 4 lead showed sinus. Exam found small lacerations to left forearm and pain on palpation
to left:knee. Patient requested transport to UKMC and was transported with advance notification given to hospital. During transport patient was given
supportive care and monitored with no new complaints or changes. Report given and care transferred to ED nursing staff. EC11 available.

Clinical indication
Pain
Comparison
None.
Findings
There isno acute fracture or dislocation. No there are moderate degenerative changes with joint space narrowing
and osteophytosis. There is deformity of the proximal fibula likely related toprior fracture.
There isan area ofsclerosis within the right lateral proximal tibia measuring approximately 2.5 cm. This could
represent a bone island. However, metastatic lesion could also have this appearance. Bone scan may be helpful for
further evaluation.
Impression
No acute fracture or dislocation.
Sclerotic focus inthe right knee. Bone scan may be helpful.
Site 1
JOSHUA A PHELPS MD

REASON FOR EXAM: LEFT UPPER QUADRANT PAIN
REASON FOR VISIT: LEFT UPPER QUADRANT PAIN
ACCESSION # CTD202401 11-0019
Procedure
CT of the abdomen and pelvis without IV contrast
Clinical indication
Left upper quadrant pain
Technique
DLP:889.21, mGy.cm CTDIvol Mean:15.6, mGy Effective Dose:13.3, mSv
5 mm thick contiguous axial images were obtained through the abdomen and pelvis without IV contrast. Multiplanar
reformats were also performed. Automated exposure control was utilized to optimize dose reduction.
Comparison
[DOB-29]
Findings
The liver, spleen, adrenals, pancreas, kidneys, urinary bladder, and uterus are unremarkable.
Moderate to large amount of stool is seen throughout the colon suggesting constipation.
Appendix is normal. Urinary bladder and uterus also are unremarkable. No free fluid or pneumoperitoneum is seen.
Stomach and small bowel are unremarkable. In the subcutaneous tissues of the anterior right lower abdomen there is
a 1cm nodule which probably represents a sebaceous cyst.
Impression
Constipation
Otherwise no acute process
Site 1

Mohammad F Shahzad, MD

History of Present IlIness
HPI
Patient pleasant 51-year-old lady who is here patient is been seen in the office couple weeks ago and was sent for a CT
scan patient been seen on the 11th she had a ventral hernia repair in 2018 and was here today with concern for a
potential recurrent hernia. On the CT scan there was no evidence recurrent hernia. However she did have large stool
burden concerning for constipation. We discussed the patient everything looks like itwas healing up well and there is no
sign ofany recurrence of her hernia. Patient was relieved to hear this. We also discussed with the patient that she does
have a large large stool burden in her colon consistent with constipation we gave her some counseling and guidance
about MiraLAX usage and we told her she otherwise is feeling well that would make her as needed to see us we told her
she any questions or concerns to call and let us know patient was relieved to hear that she did not need any emergent or
urgent surgery at this point.

Hernia
Additional details
51 Year old female here for consultation for hernia recurrent. Pain after car wreck.
Primary Care Provider
Mohammad F Shahzad, MD
General Appearance: Alert and oriented, Comfortable
History of Present Illness
HPI
Patient 51-year-old lady is here with concern for recurrent hemia. Patient had a robotic assisted laparoscopic repair of
incarcerated ventral hernia with mesh back in February 2018. Patient most recently had seen my partner Dr. Kloss for
colonoscopy and other issues but is here today to discuss potential recurrent hernia. Patient has had a car wreck
recently and been having more pain at the site from her hernia repair. Patient the wreck was back in June. Patient says
she has not had a CT since the wreck says she was T-boned and hadalot of discomfort patient says since that time she
has been having alot of discomfort that wraps around her flank and side on the left side she has not necessarily noticed a
bulge but with her body habitus this is not something that would be easily apparent.
On exam today her abdomen is soft itis tender to palpation in the left upper quadrant there with her body habitus is very
difficult totell ifshe has a hernia recurrence or not | would like to get her sent over for CT scan to look at this area. We
have set up for a CT of the abdomen pelvis without contrast to get a look at herventral abdominal wall we told herthat if
there is no hernia in the site her pain may be coming from musculoskeletal issues potentially with her back and she may
need to get into see someone about her thoracic spine. We did scan ordered we will see her back in 2 weeks.

EXAMINATION: MRI ofthe right knee with and without contrast.
SEDATION: None.
HISTORY: Anterior rightknee pain x1+ years, worse in the last eight montha since MVA. Anterior swelling, numbness,
weakness, popping/grinding, and stiffness with limited range of motion. Follow Lip mass seen on priar imaging.
COMPARISON: Comparison is made with the prior MRI from [DOB-68] and nuclear medicine study from [DOB-69].
TECHNIQUE: MRI of the right knee with and without contrast using the 3 tesla magnet. Axial proton densily with fat
saturation and T1 weighted with fat saturation, sagittal proton density and T2 welghted with fat saturation, and coronal
proton density with fat saturation, T1 weighted, and oblique T2 weighted through the ACL sequences were obtained.
A dose of 22mL of Dotarem was intravenously administered. Following the injection, axial, sagittal, and coronal T1
welghted with fat saturation sequences were performed.
FINDINGS: The anterlor and posterior cruclate ligaments are Intact, There is complex tearing involving ihe posterior
horn, posterior root, and body of the medial meniscus. There Is moderate chondromalacla within the medial
compartment. The lateral meniscus is grossly normal in signal and morphology. The medial and lateral collateral
ligamentous complexes are Intact. There Is mild-to-moderate chondromalacia within the lateral compartment with
areas offull-thickness carlllago loss and a suspected Internal osteophyte formation measuring 0.5 cm. Theextensor
mechanism is tendinopathic. There are savare arthritic changes in the palellofemoral compartment with complete
loss of articular cartilage seen along the lateral facet with extensive subchondral and/or osteochondral-type defects.
There is osteophytosis. There Is a small suprapatellar joint effusion. Post contrast Images were obtained. There are no
abnormal enhancing mass lesions detected.
There Is an Itreguiarity with fragmentation ofthe lateral tiblal plateau which Is presumably asequela of prior
comminuted depressed tibial plateau fracture. Thereis mild cortical irregularity along the articular surface ofthe lateral
tibial plateau. Thereis avery ow T1 and T2 signal lesion within the lateral epiphysis and metaphysis of the lateral
tlbla measuring approximately 1.7 cm in mediolateral dimension x 1.9 cm in craniocaudal dimension x 1.5 om in AP
dimension.
There Is « ganglion cyst formation noted atthe gastrocnemius muscle attachments, larger on the lateral attachment,
meaauring over 2 cm, There is no associated enhancement.
IMPRESSION:
1. There Is posttraumatic deformity of the lateral tibial plateau with suspected chronic comminuted fracturing. Thera Is
persistent fragmentation. There are arthiltlc changes Inthe lateral compartment with asmall Internal osteophyte. There
|s a very low T1 and T2 signal lesion within the lateral tibia. Correlation with any prior imaging is needed if available,
Ablastic lesion could potentially have a simiar appearance. Radiographic correlation is needed and correlation with
anymore recent imaging, If avaliable, There was asymmetric uptake within the right proximal tiblal metaphyseal region
onthe prior nuclear medicine study, which could also be related tothe fracture and arthritic changes vs. the lasion. A
photopenic defect was not visualized on the nuclear medicine study.
2. Thereare advanced arthritic changes In the medial compartment with moderate chondromalacia.

HISTORY: Right knee pain. Height - 5°7°, weight —240 Ibs, BMI — 37.6, Gender - Female.
COMPARISON: No prior imaging Is available althis time for comparison.
TECHNIQUE: Nuclear medicine limited bone scan was obtained after Injection of 25.9 mCi of Te99m MDP at 1352
hours. Imaging afthe knees was obtained.
FINDINGS: Smallfield ofview imaging ofthe knees ware obtalned. There Is uptake noted about the knees bilaterally.
In the left knee, there is a focal area of yptake seen along the medial tibial plateau and a larger area along the lateral
femoral condyle. In the right knee, there s more diffuse uptake seen about the knee articulations both centrally along
the periphery. There is also diffuse Involvement ofthe patella.
IMPRESSION: There is multifocal abnormal uptake aboul the knees bilaterally, asymmetrically worse on the right.
There isasymmetric uptake within the left femoral condyle and the right patella In particular.For further evaluation, MAI
imaging could be obtained. Comparison with recent prior imaging is also recommended if available.

Clinical Notes (continued)
Interval History:
Patient presents for follow up evaluation of her diabetic neuropathy. At her last visit, |transitioned her from
gabapentin to Lyrica. Pt tolerates the pregabalin but does feel that the current dose is enough. No
complaints/concerns.
ODI (4/16/24): 22
History of Present Illness:
Caryn Leigh McCay is a 51 y.o. female with uncontrolled DM2 {(A1c 13-14%) presents for neuropathic pain. Referral
from Wound Care (Sharon Pelfrey APRN) for evaluation. Reports that she has been struggling with diabetes control
and her neuropathy has worsened over the past year affecting her bilateral feet. Gabapentin was started by ID for
neuropathic pain, but she says she isstruggling with the sedative component. Mentions that following a car wreck in
2016 she was on Lyrica 75 mg twice daily for neuropathic pain from her spine that she took for a year with no issues.
Patient reportsthat she fell within the last week and states she was given Tramadol and Flexeril for which she isalmost
out, but reports that they have helped her back pain.
Site: bilateral feet
Onset: 1year ago
Severity: 5/10
Descriptors: Constant
Aggravating Factors: Walking, prolonged standing, from sitting for prolonged periodsto standing
RelievingFactors: Gabapentin
Associated Symptoms: Denies
ODI: 22
Current Disabilities:
Functional Goals of Treatment: Return to a physically active lifestyle
Current Medication:

for muscle spasms.

for mild pain.

History of Present Illness:
Caryn Leigh McCay is a 51 y.o. female with uncontrolled DM2 (Aic 13-14%) presents for neuropathic pain. Referral
from Wound Care (Sharon Pelfrey APRN) for evaluation. Reports that she has been struggling with diabetes control
and her neuropathy has worsened over the past year affecting her bilateral feet. Gabapentin was started by ID for
neuropathic pain, but she says she isstruggling with the sedative component. Mentions that following a car wreck
in 2016 she was on Lyrica 75 mg twice daily for neuropathic pain from her spine that she took for a year with no
issues, Patient reports that she fell within the last week and states she was given Tramadol and Flexeril for which she
is almost out, but reports that they have helped her back pain.
Site: bilateral feet
Onset:1 year ago
Severity: 5/10
Descriptors: Constant
Aggravating Factors: Walking, prolonged standing, from sitting for prolonged periods to standing
RelievingFactors: Gabapentin
Associated Symptoms: Denies
ODI: 22
Current Disabilities:
Functional Goals of Treatment: Return to a physically active lifestyle
Current Medication:

for muscle spasms.
ibuprofen 800 MGtablet Take 1tablet (800 mg) by mouth every6(six) hours if needed
for mild pain.
meloxicam (Mobic) 15 MG tablet Take 0.5 tablets (7.5 mg) by mouth 1 (one) time each day.
pregabalin (Lyrica) 50 MG capsule Take 1capsule (50 mg) by mouth 2 (two) times a day.
traMADol ER (Ultram-ER) 100 MG 24 hr —‘ Take by mouth. Do not crush, chew, or split.

& HealthCare
Understanding Body Mass Index (BMI)
Body mass index (BMI) is a way tofigure out your weight category. Ituses the ratio of your height to your weight.
BMI is a measure ofyour weight that Is corrected for height. Knowing your BMI is a way totell if you are ata
healthy weight, underweight, or overweight. The higher your BMI, the greater your risk forweight-related health
problems.
Why we checkyourBMI (or Why we screen forweight)
In the clinic, we screen for signs ofcommon health problems, We do this for 2reasons. First, we want tostop them.
before they start. Second, ifthey do happen, we want to treat them early. Some common problems we took forare
cancer, depression, tobacco use, diabetes and weight.
There is a lot ofdebate about BMI or weight screening. Some people sayitdoes not help todiscuss weight with
patients, But if a patient hasaBMI greater than 25 kg/m or less than 18.5 kg/m2, we have todiscuss it with them.
‘When we do this, we do notjust focus on a number ona scale. We look atthe patient's overall health, including
emotions, nutrition, social and spiritual health. Our focus is on day- to-day quality oflife. We want tohelp you learn
what makes youfeel rested, energized and healthy.
The hurnan body comes on all shapes and sizes. Your total weight is not the whole story. We must look atother
health factors. These include blood pressure, blood sugar, cholesterol, and how well you can move orexercise.
Do you have questions or concerns about this or other health issues? If so, please schedule a visit with your health
care provider.
It’s importantto be at a healthy weight. Knowing your body mass index (BMI) can help you decide ifyour weight is
healthy, or if you need tomake some changes.
BMI is a measure of how much you weigh compared tohow tall you are. Children's BMis are also based on their age
and gender.
Having a high BMI can cause many health problems like high blood pressure, diabetes, heart disease, sleep apnea,
asthma, joint problems, headaches, liver and gallbladder disease, reflux, constipation, depression, and low self
esteem.
Reducing your BMI through weight loss, healthy eating, and getting regular exercise can lead to many health
benefits.

‘What BMI means for adults
+BMI below 18.5: Underweight
+BMI 18.5 to 249: Healthy weight or ideal body weight
* BMI 25 to29.9: Overweight
+BMI 30 and over: Obese
+BMI 40 and over: Severe obesity
Use atool
Find your BMI with an online BMI calculator tool, such as this one from the CDC:
*BMI calculator foradults
Using the BMt chart
To figure out your BMI, find your height and weight (or the numbers closest tothem) on the table below. Follow each
column ofnumbers towhere your height and weight meet on thetable. That is your BMI.

New Patient Note
Subjective:
Referring Physician: Pelfrey, Sharon J, APRN
135 E Maxwell & Ste 318
Lexington, KY 40508-2678
Record Review: |personally reviewed Wound Care records
Chief Complaint: neuropathy
History of Present Illness:
Caryn Leigh McCay isa 51 y.o. female with uncontrolled DM2 (A1c 13-14%) presents forneuropathic pain. Referral
from Wound Care (Sharon Pelfrey APRN) for evaluation. Reports that she has been struggling with diabetes control
and her neuropathy has worsened over the past year affecting her bilateral feet. Gabapentin was started by ID for
neuropathic pain, but she says she is struggling with the sedative component. Mentions that following a car wreck in
2016 she was on Lyrica 75 mg twice daily for neuropathic pain from her spine that she took for a year with no issues.
Patient reports that she fell within the last week and states she was given Tramadol and Flexeril for which she is almost
out, but reports that they have helped her back pain.
Site: bilateral feet
Onset:1 year ago
Severity: 5/10
Descriptors: Constant
Aggravating Factors: Walking, prolonged standing, from sitting for prolonged periods to standing
RelievingFactors: Gabapentin
Associated Symptoms: Denies
ODI: 22
Current Disabilities:
Functional Goals of Treatment: Return to a physically active lifestyle
Current Medication:
Current Pain Medications
aspirin 81 MG EC tablet 81 mg.
cyclobenzaprine (Flexeril) 10 MG tablet Take 1tablet (10 mg) by mouth3 (three) times a day if needed
for muscle spasms.

Assessment & Plan:
Caryn Leigh McCay is a 51 y.o. female
#Neuropathy, Chronic Worsening
-2/2 uncontrolled DM2
-Gabapentin 100 mg TID was started by ID for neuropathic pain, but has been struggling with the sedation. Switch
today to Lyrica50 mg BID, which represents a doubling of current dose, but patient reports better tolerance given past
experience.
-RTCin 1 month to reassess response to change in medications outlined above
#Diabetes Mellitus Type 2
-Alc: 12.8 (12/26/23)
-complicated by poor healing gluteal, foot wounds (follows with Wound Care)
-Need to bejudicious with steroid injections given risk of potential hyperglycemia
-Patient has appointment to establish with Endocrinology for better BG control

History of Present Iliness:
Caryn Leigh McCay isa 51 y.o. female with uncontrolled DM2 (A1c 13-14%) presents forneuropathic pain. Referral
from Wound Care (Sharon Pelfrey APRN) for evaluation. Reports that she has been struggling with diabetes control
and her neuropathy has worsened over the past year affecting her bilateral feet. Gabapentin was started by ID for
neuropathic pain, but she says she isstruggling with the sedative component. Mentions that following a car wreck
in 2016 she was on Lyrica 75 mgtwice daily for neuropathic pain from her spine that she took for a year with no
issues. Patient reports that she fell within the last week and states she was given Tramadol and Flexeril for which she
is almost out, but reports thatthey have helped her back pain.
Site: bilateral feet
Onset:1year ago
Severity: 5/10
Descriptors: Constant
Aggravating Factors: Walking, prolonged standing, from sitting for prolonged periods to standing

Assessment & Plan:
Caryn Leigh McCay is a 51 y.o. female
#Neuropathy, Chronic Worsening

past experience.
-RTC in 1 month to reassess response to change in medications outlined above
#Diabetes Mellitus Type 2

Understanding Body Mass Index (BMI)
Body mass index (BMI) is a waytofigure out your weight category. It uses the ratio ofyour height toyour weight. The
BMI is a measure ofyour weight that is corrected for height. Knowing your BMI is a waytotell ifyou are at a healthy
weight, are underweight, or overweight. The higher your BMI, the greater your risk for weight-related health problems.
‘What BMI means for adults
* BMI below 18.5: Underweight
+BMI 185 to 249; Healthy weight or ideal body weight
* BMI 25 to29.9: Overweight
* BMI 30 and over: Obese
* BMI 40 and over: Severe obesity
Find your BMI with an online BMI calculator tool, such as these from the COC:
. 8Mcalculator foradults
Using the BMI chart
To figure out your BMI, find your height and weight {orthe numbers closest tothem) on the table below. Follow each
column ofnumbers towhere your height and weight meet on the table. That is your BMI.

Weight Management: Healthy Eating
Food is your body's fuel. You can’t live without it. The key is togive your body enough nutrients and energy without
eating too much. Reading food labels can help you make healthy choices. Also, learn new eating habits tomanage your
weight. Nutrition labels are being redesigned bythe FDA to emphasize the number ofcalories being consumed as well
as the amount ofmore nutrients, such as added sugars, vitamin D, and potassium.
Nutrition Facts)
8 servings per container
Serving size 2/3 cup (88g)

Eat less fat
A gram offat has almost 2.5 times the caloriesof a gram of protein or carbohydrates, Try tobalance your food choices.
50 that only 20% to35% ofyour calories comes from total fat, This means an average of2' to3% grams offat for each
100 calories you eat.

Eat more fiber
High-fiber foods are digested more slowly than low-fiber foods, so you feel full longer. Try toget at least 25 grams of
fiber each day fora2000 calorie diet. Foods high in fiber include:
* Vegetables and fruits
* Whole-grain or bran breads, pastas, and cereals
» Legumes (beans) and peas
As you start toeat more fiber, be sure todrink plentyofwater. Itwill help keep your digestive system working smoothly.
Tips
Do's and don'ts include:
» Eat a variety offoods, not just a few favorites.
* Ifyou find yourself eating when you're not hungry, ask yourselfwhy. Many ofus eat when we're bored, stressed, or
just tobe polite. Listen toyour body. Ifyou're not hungry, get busy doing something else instead ofeating.
* Eat slower, shooting for 20 to30 minutes for each meal. Ittakes 20 minutes for your stomach totell your brain
that it's full. Slow eaters tend toeat less and are still satisfied, while fast eaters may tend tobe avereaters.
* Pay attention towhat you eat. Don’t read or watch TV during your meal,

Subjective:
Caryn mccay was seen in our office on [DOB-243] for the purpose of consultation, examination
and treatment.
Ms Caryn mecay entered the office today for complaint(s) resulting from automobile vs
automobile incident and has completed the patient intake questionnaire. The questionnaire was
reviewed by the examining provider and is available in the patient's permanent digital file for
review. She signed consent for evaluation and possible treatment of injuries sustained as the
result of the accident that occurred on or about [DOB-244].
Mechanism of Injury:
Ms mccay was a driver of a midsize car (3001-3500 Ibs) while the other vehicle was described as
a midsize car (3001-3500 Ibs). When questioned about wearing seat belts, she replied she was
unrestrained. An air bag did deploy. Ms mccay confirmed the seat she was sitting in atthe time of
the collision did not break and recalled prior to impact, the headrest was in a mid position relative
to the head and the head did come in contact with the head restraint. Ms mccay reports that she
was looking straight ahead at the time of the impact. Ms mccay did strike front of head against
the dashboard, the window, and the steering wheel. Patient related she did receive a head injury
and did lose consciousness.
The patient's vehicle impact location was on the front center. The patient's vehicle movement
was moving forward. Estimated speed of patient's vehicle was moving at an increased speed
(betwee40 and 65 MPH). The damage assessment of the patient's vehicle was heavy visible
damage. The other vehicle's movement was described as turning right with an estimated speed
noted as moving slowly (between 15 and 25 MPH). Estimated damage assessment of the other
vehicle was heavy visible damage. The patient's vehicle was towed from the scene.
Police did arrive atthe scene and an accident report was completed. EMS was at the scene. Ms
mecay was driven to hospital from the scene and the following has occurred: examined, admitted
to the hospital, x-rayed, and released that day. Patient complains at the time of the accident she
felt heavy, stabbing, stiffness, and throbbingat the head, chest, front of left knee, and front of right
knee and supplemental complaints of chest pain, muscle spasm, numbness and tingling,
stomach pain, soreness, stress, shock, and headaches. Ms mccay states that since the date of
the accident the overall condition and complaints have elicited more pain and worsened.
- Functional Deficit: explains sitting is difficult for more than 1 hour; previously she could
perform this action for 5 hours.
- Functional Deficit: explains walking is difficult for more than 1 hour; previously she could
perform this action for 6 hours.
Systems Review: during a review of systems, the patient reports the status of conditions below

SUBJECTIVE
Patient Presentation
Primary Concern: Pt returns to physical therapy for progress note after completing 6 weeks of skilled physical therapy for R
sided knee pain. Her symptoms have improved since IE as she Is able to stand/walk atwork for longer periods with less overall
pain. However, reports intermittent, sharp increases In knee pain with squatting, stairs, and working long hours at VA hospital.
Taping to knee still helps reduce the pain significantly. She is pleased with her progress to date and would like to continue
therapy to further reduce the pain, and improve her strength and function.
History of Present Condition: Pt is a 50 yofemale who presents to skilled PT for evaluation with primary c/p bilateral ankle and
knee pain which have been present since she was in an MVA on 7/30/23, during which she was t-boned and her knees hit the
dash forcefully. She reports her pain is worse R>L. She rates her current pain a 6/10, pain at worst 10/10 In last 1-2 weeks, and
pain at best 5/10 (at rest, with ice/heat, activity modification/laying down).
She reports Increased pain with prolonged standing/walking (10-20 minutes), sitting (5-10 minutes), squatting, and
yardwork/housework. She has difficulty getting up/down from the floor as well. She Is currently working 2 days a week atthe
VA after 2 months off work and plans to start full-time in 4weeks. She reports she has to lay down when shegets home from
work and sometimes while on breaks at work due to pain. She reports paln/difficulty going up/down stairs, more difficulty golng
down. Can go reciprocally up with B use of hands but goes down one step at a time when descending stairs. Shejustmoved
into herson's house which is 3 stories and she has to be able to go up and down stairs all3 levels regularly for laundry, caring
for dogs. S
Her goal with physical therapy is to reduce symptoms in order to return to prior level of function and work full-time atthe VA
(working 2 days a week right now). She was also referred to see pain specialist and chiropractor (evaluation next week). She
reports she was offwork almost 2 months, she Is now working 2days a week and goal Is to work 5 days a week starting in 1
month. She reports she had a radiograph done on her right knee and there is a tumor in her knee, which was minimally painful
before MVA.
PMH: hernia sx 2016, MVA in 2016 leading to multiple LE fractures, tumor in R knee diagnosed prior to MVA not thought to be
cancerous, dlabethic neuropathy, rectal cancer, DMIl

Diagnosis
Summary: Pt presents to PT re-eval with minor improvements in LE strength and function. She still presents with deficits in LE
strength (quads, HS), gait pattern, balance, proprioception, and tissue extensibllity. These deficits negatively impact her ability to
stand/walk long hours at work, squat, and navigate stairs without increase in knee pain or associated compensations. Reduced
pain reported after McConnelltaping to reinforce R patella and patellar tendon. At this time ptis appropriate for con't skilled PT
to address current deficits to safely return to her PLOF.

Diagnosis
Summary: Patient tolerated treatment well today with no adverse events noted. Patient tolerated manual therapy well with
complaints of TTP to R distal quadriceps and R gastro medial>lateral. Patient was able to tolerate the addition of R tiblofemoral
distraction with passive IR and flexion today, as well as prone knee flexion stretch with towel gapping. Patient was able to
tolerate all therapeutic exercises, activities, and neuromuscular re-education activities well today and required only minimal
cueing. Patient presented with good tolerance to all WB and NWB exercises and the addition of minisquats. POC to con't to
progress as tolerated, and aim to perform modified McConnelltaping to R knee on Thursday.

trains and monitors Saddle Therapeutic Chair exercises. The affected body part/body region(s)
that will be rehabilitated using this therapeutic procedure is noted below: left sacroiliac, right
sacroiliac, left posterior pelvis/hip, sacral and right posterior pelvis/hip
- Manual Therapy: myofascial release (97140) performed on the following muscle: left medial
hamstring, right medial hamstring, left tensor fascia lata, right tensor fascia lata, left gluteus
medius, right gluteus medius, left quadratus lumborum, and right quadratus lumborum for 10
minutes. The goal of this therapy is to reduce attendant symptomology, as measure by the Visual
Analog Scale and tracked using the Functional Outcomes Assessment Tool, increase joint
ranges of motion, reduce or eliminate soft tissue swelling, inflammation, and restriction. This
therapy was performed by the Therapist and supervised by the Doctor: Dr. Nicholas Leverette,
DC
- As per treatment plan - Traction: Y-Axis mechanical traction performed to upper to mid
cervical for 8 minutes.
- Therapeutic Exercise (97110): Whole body vibration. Therapeutic Exercises were
performed as detailed in the initial treatment plan for 12 minutes.
The goal of this therapy is to increase functional capacity, improve posture, increase muscle and
ligament strength to the affected body part/body region(s). Due to the complexities of the patients
condition, the Dr trains and monitors Whole Body Vibration therapeutic exercises. The patient
was instructed to perform isometric exercises to the affected body part/body region(s) listed
below: left side of neck, right side of neck, left trapezius, right posterior trapezius, left mid
thoracic, right mid thoracic, left lower thoracic, right lower thoracic, left lumbar, right lumbar, left
sacroiliac and right sacroiliac
2 lbs. of weight was used to perform these isometric exercises. A halo posture correction device
was utilized for the entirety of this therapeutic procedure. Goals noted above.
- Therapeutic Exercise-Patient consented (97110): Manual stretching Therapeutic Exercises
performed as detailed in the initial treatment plan for 10 minutes.
The goal of this therapy is to increase flexibility of the affected body region(s) noted below. Due to
the complexities of the patient's condition, the Dr trains and monitors Manual Stretching
exercises. Manual stretching exercises were performed using the precor stretching equipment
and targeted to the following body part/body region(s): left lumbar, lumbar and right lumbar
- Advised
- TX Effect: Treatment rendered without incident and responding as expected
- Next Visit: continue with treatment plan as scheduled

Diagnosis

Subjective:
The patient indicates they have discomfort in the mid back. Since her last visit she reports feeling
better. She describes the discomfort as shooting, tightness, and stiffness. She is able to perform
job duties with difficulty. The following activities are limited due to the condition: lifting, bending,
standing, walking, sitting, grasping, twisting, and exercising. Discomfort increases with
movement, sitting, and standing. Discomfort decreases with rest, chiropractic care, and
medication. She experiences discomfort frequently. Severity of discomfort is described as
moderate. The patient provided the following additional information: became better with
adjustment
The patient indicates they have discomfort inthe low back. Since her last visit she reports feeling
better. She describes the discomfort as sharp, tightness, and stiffness. She is able to perform job
duties with some difficulty. The following activities are limited due to the condition: lifting, bending,
standing, walking, sitting, and exercising. Discomfort increases with movement, sitting, and
standing. Discomfort decreases with rest, chiropractic care, and medication. She experiences
discomfort frequently. Severity of discomfort is described as moderate. The patient provided the
following additional information: No
The patient indicates they have discomfort in the right knee. Since her last visit she reports
feeling better. She describes the discomfort as sharp, aching, shooting, tightness, throbbing, and
stiffness. She is able to perform job duties with some difficulty. The following activities are limited
due to the condition: lifting, bending, standing, walking, sitting, climbing stairs, and exercising.
Discomfort increases with movement and applied pressure. Discomfort decreases with rest,
chiropractic care, and medication. She experiences discomfort frequently. Severity of discomfort
is described as moderate. The patient provided the following additional information: No
Based on the patient's exam findings, we conducted a physical performance evaluation. (97750)
(45mins) A series of computerized ROM evaluations and a series of computerized muscle
strength evaluations. For results of the re-exams, see PPT form.

Today's Treatment:
- Chief Complaint: upper to mid cervical and upper lumbar
- Chiropractic Spinal Adjustments: Diversified and Drop Table to the following spinal levels:
atlas, C4, C7, T3, T7, L1, L2, and sacrum -- -- --
- Supportive Therapy to optimize treatment effectiveness the following therapy(s) were
performed today:
- Therapeutic Exercise (97110): Saddle Therapeutic Chair Therapeutic Exercises performed
as detailed in the initial treatment plan for 5 minutes
The goal of this therapy is to increase strength endurance and range of motion to the affected
body part/body region(s) noted below. Due to the complexities of the patient's condition, the Dr
trains and monitors Saddle Therapeutic Chair exercises. The affected body part/body region(s)
that will be rehabilitated using this therapeutic procedure is noted below: left sacroiliac, right
sacroiliac, left posterior pelvis/hip, sacral and right posterior pelvis/hip
- Manual Therapy: myofascial release (97140) performed on the following muscle: left medial
hamstring, right medial hamstring, left tensor fascia lata, right tensor fascia lata, left gluteus
medius, right gluteus medius, left quadratus lumborum, and right quadratus lumborum for 10
minutes. The goal of this therapy is to reduce attendant symptomology, as measure by the Visual
Analog Scale and tracked using the Functional Outcomes Assessment Tool, increase joint
ranges of motion, reduce or eliminate soft tissue swelling, inflammation, and restriction. This
therapy was performed by the Therapist and supervised by the Doctor: Dr. Nicholas Leverette,
DC
- As per treatment plan - Traction: Y-Axis mechanical traction performed to upper to mid
cervical for 8 minutes.
- Therapeutic Exercise (97110): Whole body vibration. Therapeutic Exercises were
performed as detailed in the initial treatment plan for 12 minutes.
The goal of this therapy is to increase functional capacity, improve posture, increase muscle and
ligament strength to the affected body part/body region(s). Due to the complexities of the patients
condition, the Dr trains and monitors Whole Body Vibration therapeutic exercises. The patient
was instructed to perform isometric exercises to the affected body part/body region(s) listed
below: left side of neck, right side of neck, left trapezius, right posterior trapezius, left mid
thoracic, right mid thoracic, left lower thoracic, right lower thoracic, left lumbar, right lumbar, left
sacroiliac and right sacroiliac

The patient indicates they have discomfort in the low back. She describes the discomfort as dull,
aching, tightness, throbbing, and stiffness. Since her last visit she reports feeling the same.

chiropractic care, and medication. She experiences discomfort frequently. Severity of discomfort
is described as moderate. Her typical or average pain is rated 6 out of 10, with 10 being the worst
pain possible. She is able to perform job duties with some difficulty. The following activities are
limited due to the condition: lifting, bending, standing, walking, sitting, pulling, pushing, twisting,

ASSESSMENT
Diagnosis
Summary: Patient tolerated treatment well today. Addition of more CKC and weight bearing activities were introduced in which
the patient tolerated well with minimal cueing required. These included bosu lunges, one arm rows with LE activation at cable
column, and one arm serratus punches with LE activation at cable columns as well. Patient still presents with deviations in gait,
deficits in LE strength R>L, and tissue extensibility R>L. Hypomobility ofthe R femoralacetabulr joint was noted today during LAD
and lateral distraction of the Joint.Continuation of skilled therapy Is warranted to address these deficits In order to meettherapy
goals and return to PLOF.
See code details above

1. Problem: strength, ROM, galt, function/ADLs

- Therapeutic Exercise (97110): Saddle Therapeutic Chair Therapeutic Exercises performed
as detailed in the initial treatment plan for 5 minutes
The goal of this therapy is to increase strength endurance and range of motion to the affected
body part/body region(s) noted below. Due to the complexities of the patient's condition, the Dr
trains and monitors Saddle Therapeutic Chair exercises. The affected body part/body region(s)
that will be rehabilitated using this therapeutic procedure is noted below: left sacroiliac, right
sacroiliac, left posterior pelvis/hip, sacral and right posterior pelvis/hip
- Manual Therapy: myofascial release (97140) performed on the following muscle: left medial
hamstring, right medial hamstring, left tensor fascia lata, right tensor fascia lata, left gluteus
medius, right gluteus medius, left quadratus lumborum, and right quadratus lumborum for 10
minutes. The goal of this therapy is to reduce attendant symptomology, as measure by the Visual
Analog Scale and tracked using the Functional Outcomes Assessment Tool, increase joint
ranges of motion, reduce or eliminate soft tissue swelling, inflammation, and restriction. This
therapy was performed by the Therapist and supervised by the Doctor: Dr. Nicholas Leverette,
DC
- As per treatment plan - Traction: Y-Axis mechanical traction performed to upper to mid
cervical for 8 minutes.
- Therapeutic Exercise (97110): Whole body vibration. Therapeutic Exercises were
performed as detailed in the initial treatment plan for 12 minutes.
The goal of this therapy is to increase functional capacity, improve posture, increase muscle and
ligament strength to the affected body part/body region(s). Due to the complexities of the patients
condition, the Dr trains and monitors Whole Body Vibration therapeutic exercises. The patient
was instructed to perform isometric exercises to the affected body part/body region(s) listed
below: left side of neck, right side of neck, left trapezius, right posterior trapezius, left mid
thoracic, right mid thoracic, left lower thoracic, right lower thoracic, left lumbar, right lumbar, left
sacroiliac and right sacroiliac
2 Ibs. of weight was used to perform these isometric exercises. A halo posture correction device
was utilized for the entirety of this therapeutic procedure. Goals noted above.
- Therapeutic Exercise-Patient consented (97110): Manual stretching Therapeutic Exercises
performed as detailed in the initial treatment plan for 10 minutes.
The goal of this therapy is to increase flexibility of the affected body region(s) noted below. Due to
the complexities of the patient's condition, the Dr trains and monitors Manual Stretching
exercises. Manual stretching exercises were performed using the precor stretching equipment

Start Time:10:30am EST End Time: 11:30am EST Place of Service: 11— Office
SUBJECTIVE
Patient Presentation
Primary Concern: Pt returns to physical therapy for progress note after completing 4weeks of skilled physical therapy for R
sided knee pain. She states hersymptoms have improved since IE as she Is able to stand/walk atwork for longer periods with
less overall pain. Knee pain still increases when squatting, using stairs, and pushing/pulling/lifting items at work. Taping herknee
helps reduce the pain significantly at home and work. She is please with her progress to date and would like to continue therapy
to further reduce the pain, and improve her strength and function.
History of Present Condition: Pt is a 50 yofemale who presents to skilled PT for evaluation with primary c/p bilateral ankle and
knee pain which have been present since she was in an MVA on 7/30/23, during which she was t-boned and her knees hit the
dash forcefully. She reports her pain is worse R>L. She rates her current pain a 6/10, pain at worst 10/10 In last 1-2 weeks, and
pain at best 5/10 (at rest, with ice/heat, activity modification/laying down).
She reports Increased pain with prolonged standing/walking (10-20 minutes), sitting (5-10 minutes), squatting, and
yardwork/housework. She has difficulty getting up/down from the floor as well. She Is currently working 2 days a week atthe
VA after 2 months off work and plans to start full-time in 4weeks. She reports she has to lay down when shegets home from
work and sometimes while on breaks at work due to pain. She reports paln/difficulty going up/down stairs, more difficulty golng
down. Can go reciprocally up with B use of hands but goes down one step at a time when descending stairs. Shejustmoved
into herson's house which is 3 stories and she has to be able to go up and down stairs all3 levels regularly for laundry, caring
for dogs. S
Her goal with physical therapy is to reduce symptoms in order to return to prior level of function and work full-time atthe VA
(working 2 days a week right now). She was also referred to see pain specialist and chiropractor (evaluation next week). She
reports she was offwork almost 2 months, she Is now working 2days a week and goal Is to work 5 days a week starting in 1
month. She reports she had a radiograph done on her right knee and there is a tumor in her knee, which was minimally painful
before MVA.
PMH: hernia sx 2016, MVA in 2016 leading to multiple LE fractures, tumor in R knee diagnosed prior to MVA not thought to be
cancerous, dlabethic neuropathy, rectal cancer, DMIl
Date of Injury/Onset: [DOB-426]
Timing ofthe Problem: Subacute
Startof Care: [DOB-427]
Treatment Side: Right
Patientl/Caregiver Goals: decrease pain and restore function
Selfperceived Progress Toward Goal: 40%
Pain Presentation
Pain Location(s): R knee
Current Pain Level: 4
Pain Description: aching, sharp
Alleviating Factors: rest, tape, exercise
Exacerbating Factors: repetitive activity (squatting, stairs, lifting, carrying)

OBJECTIVE
Standardized Tests
Lower Extremity Functional Scale
Description: The Lower Extremity Functional Scale Is used to evaluate the Impairment of a patient with lower extremity
musculoskeletal condition or disorders.
Please rate your pain level with activity. (5)
1: Any of your usual work, housework, or school activities.Moderate difficulty. (2)
2:Your usual hobbies, recreational, or sporting activities. Moderate difficulty. (2)
3: Getting into or out ofthe bath.A little bit of difficulty. (3)
4:Walking between rooms.Alittle bit of difficulty. (3)
5: Putting on your shoes or socks. A little bit of difficulty. (3)
6: Squatting. Moderate difficulty. (2)
7: Lifting anobject, like a bag of groceries from the floor. No difficulty. (4)
8: Performing light activities around your home.A little bit of difficulty. (3)
9: Performing heavy activities around your home. Moderate difficulty. (2)
10: Getting Into or out of a car. A little bit of difficulty. (3)
11: Walking 2 blocks.A little bit of difficulty. (3)
12: Walking a mile. Moderate difficulty. (2)
13: Going up or down 10 stairs (about 1flight of stairs).A little bit ofdifficulty. (3)
14: Standing for 1hour. A little bit of difficulty. (3)
15: Sitting for 1hour. A little bit of difficulty. (3)
16: Running on even ground. Quitea bitofdifficulty. (1)
17: Running on uneven ground. Quitea bitof difficulty. (1)
18: Making sharp turns while running fast. Quite a bit ofdifficulty. (1)
19: Hopping. Moderate difficulty. (2)
20: Rolling over in bed.A little bitof difficulty. (3)
Total Score: 49 /80
Additional Comments: 67.5% disability
Outcomes Disability/Symptom Score49/80
Oswestry Disability Index
The Oswestry Disability Index is an extremely important tool that researchers and disability evaluators useto measure a patient's
functional disability.
Section1 - Pain Intensity: The pain is moderate atthe moment. (2)
Section 6 - Standing: Pain prevents me from standing more than 1hour. (2)
Section 2- Personal Care (Washing, Dressing, etc): |can look after myself normally but It causes extra pain. (1)
Section 7- Sleeping: Because of pain | have less than 4 hours sleep. (3)
Section 3 - Lifting: Pain prevents me from lifting heavy weights, but | can manage light to medium weights ifthey are
conveniently positioned. (3)
Section 4 - Walking: Pain prevents me from walking more than 1/2 mile. (2)

Subjective:
The patient indicates they have discomfort in the neck. Since her last visit she reports feeling
better. She describes the discomfort as aching, tightness, and stiffness. She is able to perform
job duties with some difficulty. The following activities are limited due to the condition: sitting,
driving, and exercising. Discomfort increases with movement and sitting. Discomfort decreases
with rest, chiropractic care, and medication. She experiences discomfort intermittently. Severity of
discomfort is described as mild. Her typical or average pain is rated 4out of 10, with 10 being the
worst pain possible. The patient provided the following additional information: better
The patient indicates they have discomfort inthe low back. Since her last visit she reports feeling
better. She describes the discomfort as aching, tightness, and stiffness. She is able to perform
job duties with some difficulty. The following activities are limited due to the condition: standing,
walking, sitting, twisting, climbing stairs, driving, and exercising. Discomfort increases with
movement, applied pressure, sitting, and standing. Discomfort decreases with rest, chiropractic
care, medication, movement, and heat. She experiences discomfort intermittently. Severity of
discomfort is described as mild. Her typical or average pain is rated 4out of 10, with 10 being the
worst pain possible. The patient provided the following additional information: better
The patient indicates they have discomfort in the right knee. Since her last visit she reports
feeling the same. She describes the discomfort as sharp, aching, numbness, tightness,
throbbing, and stiffness. She is able to perform job duties with some difficulty. The following
activities are limited due to the condition: lifting, bending, standing, walking, sitting, twisting,
climbing stairs, driving, and exercising. Discomfort increases with movement, applied pressure,
sitting, and standing. Discomfort decreases with rest, chiropractic care, medication, and heat.
She experiences discomfort frequently. Severity of discomfort is described as moderate. Her
typical or average pain is rated 7 out of 10, with 10being the worst pain possible. The patient
provided the following additional information: everyday all day
Right knee had a tumor prior to the MVC- has been inflamed and may have increased in size
following the collision. Reports that she is having less pain when taped at PT. Reports increased
pain in Right calf after PT.
May have an MRI, bone graft and total knee replacement had been recommended previously.
She will inform us of the findings.

Diagnosis
Summary: Pt presents to PT re-eval with objective improvements in LE strength, ROM, functional mobility, and LEFS score. Still
presents with deficits in galt pattern, quad/HS strength R>L, and tissue extensibility R>L all ofwhich impair herability to squat,
stand, walk, navigate stairs and perform repetitive tasks without increase in RLE pain and compensations. At this time ptis
appropriate for con't skilled PT to address above objective and functional deficits to safely return to her PLOF.
See code details above

wo
