offunction and tolerance to functional tasks such as going up/down stairs, doing dishes, cooking, walking, and
getting up/down from toilet
Target Timeframe: Eight weeks

Description: Patient will Increase Ankle AROM to 10 degrees of dorsiflexion and 55 degrees of plantarflexion to
be able to perform ADL's with less compensation

- Supportive Therapy to optimize treatment effectiveness the following therapy(s) were
performed today:
- Therapeutic Exercise (97110): Saddle Therapeutic Chair Therapeutic Exercises performed
as detailed in the initial treatment plan for 5 minutes
The goal of this therapy is to increase strength endurance and range of motion to the affected
body part/body region(s) noted below. Due to the complexities of the patient's condition, the Dr
trains and monitors Saddle Therapeutic Chair exercises. The affected body part/body region(s)
that will be rehabilitated using this therapeutic procedure is noted below: left sacroiliac, right
sacroiliac, left posterior pelvis/hip, sacral and right posterior pelvis/hip
- Manual Therapy: myofascial release (97140) performed on the following muscle: left medial
hamstring, right medial hamstring, left tensor fascia lata, right tensor fascia lata, left gluteus
medius, right gluteus medius, left quadratus lumborum, and right quadratus lumborum for 10
minutes. The goal of this therapy is to reduce attendant symptomology, as measure by the Visual
Analog Scale and tracked using the Functional Outcomes Assessment Tool, increase joint
ranges of motion, reduce or eliminate soft tissue swelling, inflammation, and restriction. This
therapy was performed by the Therapist and supervised by the Doctor: Dr. Nicholas Leverette,
DC
- As per treatment plan - Traction: Y-Axis mechanical traction performed to upper to mid
cervical for 8 minutes.
- Therapeutic Exercise (97110): Whole body vibration. Therapeutic Exercises were
performed as detailed in the initial treatment plan for 12 minutes.
The goal of this therapy is to increase functional capacity, improve posture, increase muscle and
ligament strength to the affected body part/body region(s). Due to the complexities of the patients
condition, the Dr trains and monitors Whole Body Vibration therapeutic exercises. The patient
was instructed to perform isometric exercises to the affected body part/body region(s) listed
below: left side of neck, right side of neck, left trapezius, right posterior trapezius, left mid
thoracic, right mid thoracic, left lower thoracic, right lower thoracic, left lumbar, right lumbar, left
sacroiliac and right sacroiliac
2 lbs. of weight was used to perform these isometric exercises. A halo posture correction device
was utilized for the entirety of this therapeutic procedure. Goals noted above.
- Therapeutic Exercise-Patient consented (97110): Manual stretching Therapeutic Exercises
performed as detailed in the initial treatment plan for 10 minutes.

Print
- Therapeutic Exercise (97110): Saddle Therapeutic Chair Therapeutic Exercises performed
as detailed in the initial treatment plan for 5 minutes
The goal of this therapy is to increase strength endurance and range of motion to the affected
body part/body region(s) noted below. Due to the complexities of the patient's condition, the Dr
trains and monitors Saddle Therapeutic Chair exercises. The affected body part/body region(s)
that will be rehabilitated using this therapeutic procedure is noted below: left sacroiliac, right
sacroiliac, left posterior pelvis/hip, sacral and right posterior pelvis/hip
- Manual Therapy: myofascial release (97140) performed on the following muscle: left medial
hamstring, right medial hamstring, left tensor fascia lata, right tensor fascia lata, left gluteus
medius, right gluteus medius, left quadratus lumborum, and right quadratus lumborum for 10
minutes. The goal of this therapy is to reduce attendant symptomology, as measure by the Visual
Analog Scale and tracked using the Functional Outcomes Assessment Tool, increase joint
ranges of motion, reduce or eliminate soft tissue swelling, inflammation, and restriction. This
therapy was performed by the Therapist and supervised by the Doctor: Dr. Nicholas Leverette,
DC
- As per treatment plan - Traction: Y-Axis mechanical traction performed to upper to mid
cervical for 8 minutes.
- Therapeutic Exercise (97110): Whole body vibration. Therapeutic Exercises were
performed as detailed in the initial treatment plan for 12 minutes.
The goal of this therapy is to increase functional capacity, improve posture, increase muscle and
ligament strength to the affected body part/body region(s). Due to the complexities of the patients
condition, the Dr trains and monitors Whole Body Vibration therapeutic exercises. The patient
was instructed to perform isometric exercises to the affected body part/body region(s) listed
below: left side of neck, right side of neck, left trapezius, right posterior trapezius, left mid
thoracic, right mid thoracic, left lower thoracic, right lower thoracic, left lumbar, right lumbar, left
sacroiliac and right sacroiliac
2 Ibs. of weight was used to perform these isometric exercises. A halo posture correction device
was utilized for the entirety of this therapeutic procedure. Goals noted above.
- Therapeutic Exercise-Patient consented (97110): Manual stretching Therapeutic Exercises
performed as detailed in the initial treatment plan for 10 minutes.
The goal of this therapy is to increase flexibility of the affected body region(s) noted below. Due to
the complexities of the patient's condition, the Dr trains and monitors Manual Stretching
exercises. Manual stretching exercises were performed using the precor stretching equipment

Subjective:
The patient indicates they have discomfort in the neck. She describes the discomfort as
tightness, tingling, and stiffness. Since her last visit she reports feeling better. Discomfort
increases with sitting. Discomfort decreases with rest, chiropractic care, and medication. She
experiences discomfort intermittently. Severity of discomfort is described as mild. Her typical or
average pain is rated 3 out of 10, with 10 being the worst pain possible. She is able to perform
job duties with some difficulty. The following activities are limited due to the condition: sitting,
sleeping, driving, and exercising. The patient provided the following additional information: no
The patient indicates they have discomfort inthe low back. She describes the discomfort as
aching, tightness, and stiffness. Since her last visit she reports feeling better. Discomfort
increases with movement, applied pressure, sitting, and standing. Discomfort decreases with
rest, chiropractic care, and medication. She experiences discomfort intermittently. Severity of
discomfort is described as moderate. Her typical or average pain is rated 5 out of 10, with 10
being the worst pain possible. She is able to perform job duties with some difficulty. The following
activities are limited due to the condition: lifting, bending, standing, walking, sitting, climbing
Stairs, driving, and exercising. The patient provided the following additional information: no
The patient indicates they have discomfort in the right knee. She describes the discomfort as
sharp, aching, shooting, tightness, throbbing, tingling, and stiffness. Since her last visit she
reports feeling the same. Discomfort increases with movement, applied pressure, sitting, and
standing. Discomfort decreases with rest, chiropractic care, and medication. She experiences
discomfort frequently. Severity of discomfort is described as moderate. Her typical or average
pain is rated 7out of 10, with 10being the worst pain possible. She is able to perform job duties
with some difficulty. The following activities are limited due to the condition: lifting, bending,
standing, walking, sitting, twisting, climbing stairs, sleeping, driving, and exercising. The patient
provided the following additional information: no
Has been at the hospital dealing with her brother's lifé threatening injury. Has been unable to
keep up with appointments due to the family stress.
States that she wants to continue care as she is able, but is having a difficulty with the
recommended schedule.

- Supportive Therapy to optimize treatment effectiveness the following therapy(s) were
performed today:
- Therapeutic Exercise (97110): Saddle Therapeutic Chair Therapeutic Exercises performed
as detailed in the initial treatment plan for 5 minutes
The goal of this therapy is to increase strength endurance and range of motion to the affected
body part/body region(s) noted below. Due to the complexities of the patient's condition, the Dr
trains and monitors Saddle Therapeutic Chair exercises. The affected body part/body region(s)
that will be rehabilitated using this therapeutic procedure is noted below: left sacroiliac, right
sacroiliac, left posterior pelvis/hip, sacral and right posterior pelvis/hip
- Manual Therapy: myofascial release (97140) performed on the following muscle: left medial
hamstring, right medial hamstring, left tensor fascia lata, right tensor fascia lata, left gluteus
medius, right gluteus medius, left quadratus lumborum, and right quadratus lumborum for 10
minutes. The goal of this therapy is to reduce attendant symptomology, as measure by the Visual
Analog Scale and tracked using the Functional Outcomes Assessment Tool, increase joint
ranges of motion, reduce or eliminate soft tissue swelling, inflammation, and restriction. This
therapy was performed by the Therapist and supervised by the Doctor: Dr. Nicholas Leverette,
DC
- As per treatment plan - Traction: Y-Axis mechanical traction performed to upper to mid
cervical for 8 minutes.
- Therapeutic Exercise (97110): Whole body vibration. Therapeutic Exercises were
performed as detailed in the initial treatment plan for 12 minutes.
The goal of this therapy is to increase functional capacity, improve posture, increase muscle and
ligament strength to the affected body part/body region(s). Due to the complexities of the patients
condition, the Dr trains and monitors Whole Body Vibration therapeutic exercises. The patient
was instructed to perform isometric exercises to the affected body part/body region(s) listed
below: left side of neck, right side of neck, left trapezius, right posterior trapezius, left mid
thoracic, right mid thoracic, left lower thoracic, right lower thoracic, left lumbar, right lumbar, left
sacroiliac and right sacroiliac
2 lbs. of weight was used to perform these isometric exercises. A halo posture correction device
was utilized for the entirety of this therapeutic procedure. Goals noted above.
- Therapeutic Exercise-Patient consented (97110): Manual stretching Therapeutic Exercises
performed as detailed in the initial treatment plan for 10 minutes.

Subjective:
The patient indicates they have discomfort in the neck. Since her last visit she reports feeling
better. She describes the discomfort as tightness and stiffness. She is able to perform job duties
with some difficulty. The following activities are limited due to the condition: standing, walking,
sitting, driving, and exercising. Discomfort increases with movement, applied pressure, sitting,
and standing. Discomfort decreases with rest, chiropractic care, and medication. She
experiences discomfort intermittently. Severity of discomfort is described as mild. Her typical or
average pain is rated 4 out of 10, with 10 being the worst pain possible. The patient provided the
following additional information: Ok
The patient indicates they have discomfort inthe low back. Since her last visit she reports feeling
better. She describes the discomfort as aching, tightness, and stiffness. She is able to perform
job duties with some difficulty. The following activities are limited due to the condition: lifting,
bending, standing, walking, sitting, twisting, climbing stairs, sleeping, driving, and exercising.
Discomfort increases with movement, applied pressure, sitting, and standing. Discomfort
decreases with rest, chiropractic care, and medication. She experiences discomfort intermittently.
Severity of discomfort is described as moderate. Her typical or average pain is rated 6 out of 10,
with 10 being the worst pain possible. The patient provided the following additional information:
Ok
The patient indicates they have discomfort inthe right knee. Since her last visit she reports
feeling the same. She describes the discomfort as sharp, aching, numbness, tightness,
throbbing, tingling, and stiffness. She is able to perform job duties with some difficulty. The
following activities are limited due to the condition: lifting, bending, standing, walking, sitting,
sleeping, driving, and exercising. Discomfort increases with movement, applied pressure, and
sitting. Discomfort decreases with rest, chiropractic care, and medication. She experiences
discomfort intermittently. Severity of discomfort is described as moderate. Her typical or average
pain is rated 7 out of 10, with 10being the worst pain possible. The patient provided the following
additional information: Ok

Diagnosis
Summary: Pt tolerated today's treatment with minimal c/o pain and difficulty. Caryn reported increased thoracolumbar
pain/soreness R>L after visit with chiro. Performed moist heat but held on any additional tx to reduce any soreness post-tx.
Progressed program without incident. Good response to McConnell taping, performed again today with fat pad offload. Pt
noted pain in R knee with STS with 1FM pad, less with 2 FM pads (elevated surface). Cont to progress as tolerated.

Subjective:
The patient indicates they have discomfort in the neck. Since her last visit she reports feeling
better. She describes the discomfort as tightness and stiffness. She is able to perform job duties
with some difficulty. The following activities are limited due to the condition: standing, walking,
sitting, sleeping, driving, and exercising. Discomfort increases with movement, applied pressure,
sitting, and standing. Discomfort decreases with rest, chiropractic care, medication, and
movement. She experiences discomfort intermittently. Severity of discomfort is described as mild.
Her typical or average pain is rated 4 out of 10, with 10 being the worst pain possible. The patient
provided the following additional information: ok
The patient indicates they have discomfort inthe low back. Since her last visit she reports feeling
better. She describes the discomfort as aching, tightness, and stiffness. She is able to perform
job duties with some difficulty. The following activities are limited due to the condition: standing,
walking, sitting, sleeping, driving, and exercising. Discomfort increases with movement, applied
pressure, sitting, and standing. Discomfort decreases with rest, chiropractic care, medication,
and movement. She experiences discomfort intermittently. Severity of discomfort is described as
mild. Her typical or average pain is rated 5 out of 10, with 10 being the worst pain possible. The
patient provided the following additional information: ok
The patient indicates they have discomfort inthe right knee. Since her last visit she reports
feeling the same. She describes the discomfort as aching, tightness, throbbing, and stiffness.
She is able to perform job duties with some difficulty. The following activities are limited due to
the condition: lifting, bending, standing, walking, sitting, sleeping, driving, and exercising.
Discomfort increases with movement, applied pressure, and sitting. Discomfort decreases with
rest, chiropractic care, medication, and movement. She experiences discomfort intermittently.
Severity of discomfort is described as moderate. Her typical or average pain is rated 7 out of 10,
with 10 being the worst pain possible. The patient provided the following additional information:

Print
- Therapeutic Exercise (97110): Saddle Therapeutic Chair Therapeutic Exercises performed
as detailed in the initial treatment plan for 5 minutes
The goal of this therapy is to increase strength endurance and range of motion to the affected
body part/body region(s) noted below. Due to the complexities of the patient's condition, the Dr
trains and monitors Saddle Therapeutic Chair exercises. The affected body part/body region(s)
that will be rehabilitated using this therapeutic procedure is noted below: left sacroiliac, right
sacroiliac, left posterior pelvis/hip, sacral and right posterior pelvis/hip
- Manual Therapy: myofascial release (97140) performed on the following muscle: left medial
hamstring, right medial hamstring, left tensor fascia lata, right tensor fascia lata, left gluteus
medius, right gluteus medius, left quadratus lumborum, and right quadratus lumborum for 10
minutes. The goal of this therapy is to reduce attendant symptomology, as measure by the Visual
Analog Scale and tracked using the Functional Outcomes Assessment Tool, increase joint
ranges of motion, reduce or eliminate soft tissue swelling, inflammation, and restriction. This
therapy was performed by the Therapist and supervised by the Doctor: Dr. Nicholas Leverette,
- As per treatment plan - Traction: Y-Axis mechanical traction performed to upper to mid
cervical for 8 minutes.
- Therapeutic Exercise (97110): Whole body vibration. Therapeutic Exercises were
performed as detailed in the initial treatment plan for 12 minutes.
The goal of this therapy is to increase functional capacity, improve posture, increase muscle and
ligament strength to the affected body part/body region(s). Due to the complexities of the patients
condition, the Dr trains and monitors Whole Body Vibration therapeutic exercises. The patient
was instructed to perform isometric exercises to the affected body part/body region(s) listed
below: left side of neck, right side of neck, left trapezius, right posterior trapezius, left mid
thoracic, right mid thoracic, left lower thoracic, right lower thoracic, left lumbar, right lumbar, left
sacroiliac and right sacroiliac
2 Ibs. of weight was used to perform these isometric exercises. A halo posture correction device
was utilized for the entirety of this therapeutic procedure. Goals noted above.
- Therapeutic Exercise-Patient consented (97110): Manual stretching Therapeutic Exercises
performed as detailed in the initial treatment plan for 10 minutes.
The goal of this therapy is to increase flexibility of the affected body region(s) noted below. Due to
the complexities of the patient's condition, the Dr trains and monitors Manual Stretching
exercises. Manual stretching exercises were performed using the precor stretching equipment
and targeted to the following body part/body region(s): left lumbar, lumbar and right lumbar

increases with movement, applied pressure, sitting, and standing. Discomfort decreases with
rest, chiropractic care, and medication. She experiences discomfort occasionally. Severity of
discomfort is described as moderate. Her typical or average pain is rated 5 out of 10, with 10
being the worst pain possible. She is able to perform job duties with some difficulty. The following

The patient indicates they have discomfort inthe right knee. She describes the discomfort as
aching, numbness, tightness, throbbing, and stiffness. Since her last visit she reports feeling the
same. Discomfort increases with movement, applied pressure, sitting, and standing. Discomfort
decreases with rest, chiropractic care, and medication. She experiences discomfort intermittently.

following activities are limited due to the condition: lifting, bending, standing, walking, sitting,

additional information: No
Ms McCay reported that her right knee is not getting any better- it is her primary complaint now.
She reported that she has been going to physical therapy with little improvement. She also
advised that she has a tumor under her right knee cap. A surgeon recommended that she have it
removed and havea total knee replacement. She is hesitant about persuing this plan.

Intake: Caryn Is a 50 yo female who presents with primary c/o B knee and ankle pain since shewas in an MVA on 7/30/23. Her
pain is worse on the right than the left and she reports her low back pain which she had had previously has been worse since
the MVA as well. She reports radiographic findings prior to MVA showed a tumor In her right knee, and she has orders for
another radiograph ordered. Her initial functional scale score is 26/80 on LEFS and 22/50 on Oswestry, indicating severe
disability. She has pain/difficulty with functional tasks, including prolonged standing/walking, going up/down stairs (worsegoing
down than up), getting up/down from a chalr/tollet, performing household chores, and working atthe VA. She currently works 2
days per week after having 2 mos offfollowing MVA and Is applying to work 5 daysper week fulltime 4 weeks from now. Pt will
benefit from skilled PT services at this time to address impairments found and return to PML of function without pain or
limitation. Good prognosis and recovery with skilled PT Is expected atthls time.
See code details above

minutes. The goal of this therapy is to reduce attendant symptomology, as measure by the Visual
Analog Scale and tracked using the Functional Outcomes Assessment Tool, increase joint
ranges of motion, reduce or eliminate soft tissue swelling, inflammation, and restriction. This
therapy was performed by the Therapist and supervised by the Doctor: Dr. Nicholas Leverette,
DC
- As per treatment plan - Traction: Y-Axis mechanical traction performed to upper to mid
cervical for 8 minutes.
- Therapeutic Exercise (97110): Whole body vibration. Therapeutic Exercises were
performed as detailed in the initial treatment plan for 12 minutes.
The goal of this therapy is to increase functional capacity, improve posture, increase muscle and
ligament strength to the affected body part/body region(s). Due to the complexities of the patients
condition, the Dr trains and monitors Whole Body Vibration therapeutic exercises. The patient
was instructed to perform isometric exercises to the affected body part/body region(s) listed
below: left side of neck, right side of neck, left trapezius, right posterior trapezius, left mid
thoracic, right mid thoracic, left lower thoracic, right lower thoracic, left lumbar, right lumbar, left
sacroiliac and right sacroiliac
2 lbs. of weight was used to perform these isometric exercises. A halo posture correction device
was utilized for the entirety of this therapeutic procedure. Goals noted above.
- Therapeutic Exercise-Patient consented (97110): Manual stretching Therapeutic Exercises
performed as detailed in the initial treatment plan for 10 minutes.
The goal of this therapy is to increase flexibility of the affected body region(s) noted below. Due to
the complexities of the patient's condition, the Dr trains and monitors Manual Stretching
exercises. Manual stretching exercises were performed using the precor stretching equipment
and targeted to the following body part/body region(s): left lumbar, lumbar and right lumbar

SUBJECTIVE
Patient Presentation
Primary Concern: B ankle/knee pain s/p MVA on 7/30/23
History of Present Condition: Pt is a 50 yofemale who presents to skilled PT for evaluation with primary c/p bilateral ankle and
knee pain which have been present since she was in an MVA on 7/30/23, during which she was t-boned and her knees hit the
dash forcefully. She reports her pain is worse R>L. She rates her current pain a 6/10, pain atworst 10/10 in last 1-2 weeks, and
pain at best 5/10 (at rest, with ice/heat, activity modification/laying down).
She reports increased pain with prolonged standing/walking (10-20 minutes), sitting (5-10 minutes), squatting, and
yardwork/housework. She has difficulty getting up/down from the floor as well. She is currently working 2days a week atthe
VA after 2 months off work and plans to start full-time in 4weeks. She reports she has to lay down when shegets home from
work and sometimes while on breaks at work due to pain. She reports pain/difficulty going up/down stairs, more difficulty going
down. Can go reciprocally up with B use of hands but goes down one step at a time when descending stairs. She Just moved
Into her son’s house which Is 3 stories and she has to be able to go up and down stairs all3 levels regularly for laundry, caring
for dogs. S
Her goal with physical therapy Is to reduce symptoms in order to return to prior level of function and work full-time atthe VA
(working 2 days a week right now). She was also referred to see pain specialist and chiropractor (evaluation next week). She
reports she was offwork almost 2 months, she Is now working 2days a week and goal Is to work 5 days a week starting in 1
month. She reports she had a radiograph done on her right knee and there is a tumor In her knee, which was minimally painful
before MVA.
PMH: hernia sx 2016, MVA in 2016 leading to multiple LE fractures, tumor in R knee diagnosed prior to MVA not thought to be
cancerous, diabethic neuropathy, rectal cancer, DMIl
Date of Injury/Onset: [DOB-638]
Timing ofthe Problem: Subacute
Startof Care: [DOB-639]
Current Functional Limitations Impacting Prior Level of Function
Additional Comments: Current Level of Function: able to work 2 days per week, requires rest breaks at work and often lies down
when she gets home from work for rest
Prior Level of Function: independent and able to work full-time without restriction from lower extremity pain

Today's Treatment:
- Chief Complaint: upper to mid cervical and upper lumbar, right knee
- Chiropractic Spinal Adjustments: Diversified and Drop Table to the following spinal levels:
atlas, C4, C7, T3, T7, L1, L2, and sacrum -- -- --
- Supportive Therapy to optimize treatment effectiveness the following therapy(s) were
performed today:
- Therapeutic Exercise (97110): Saddle Therapeutic Chair Therapeutic Exercises performed
as detailed in the initial treatment plan for 5 minutes
The goal of this therapy is to increase strength endurance and range of motion to the affected
body part/body region(s) noted below. Due to the complexities of the patient's condition, the Dr
trains and monitors Saddle Therapeutic Chair exercises. The affected body part/body region(s)
that will be rehabilitated using this therapeutic procedure is noted below: left sacroiliac, right
sacroiliac, left posterior pelvis/hip, sacral and right posterior pelvis/hip
- Manual Therapy: myofascial release (97140) performed on the following muscle: left medial
hamstring, right medial hamstring, left tensor fascia lata, right tensor fascia lata, left gluteus
medius, right gluteus medius, left quadratus lumborum, and right quadratus lumborum for 10

minutes. The goal of this therapy is to reduce attendant symptomology, as measure by the Visual
Analog Scale and tracked using the Functional Outcomes Assessment Tool, increase joint
ranges of motion, reduce or eliminate soft tissue swelling, inflammation, and restriction. This
therapy was performed by the Therapist and supervised by the Doctor: Dr. Nicholas Leverette,
DC
- As per treatment plan - Traction: Y-Axis mechanical traction performed to upper to mid
cervical for 8 minutes.
- Therapeutic Exercise (97110): Whole body vibration. Therapeutic Exercises were
performed as detailed in the initial treatment plan for 12 minutes.
The goal of this therapy is to increase functional capacity, improve posture, increase muscle and
ligament strength to the affected body part/body region(s). Due to the complexities of the patients
condition, the Dr trains and monitors Whole Body Vibration therapeutic exercises. The patient
was instructed to perform isometric exercises to the affected body part/body region(s) listed
below: left side of neck, right side of neck, left trapezius, right posterior trapezius, left mid
thoracic, right mid thoracic, left lower thoracic, right lower thoracic, left lumbar, right lumbar, left
sacroiliac and right sacroiliac
2 lbs. of weight was used to perform these isometric exercises. A halo posture correction device
was utilized for the entirety of this therapeutic procedure. Goals noted above.
- Therapeutic Exercise-Patient consented (97110): Manual stretching Therapeutic Exercises
performed as detailed in the initial treatment plan for 10 minutes.
The goal of this therapy is to increase flexibility of the affected body region(s) noted below. Due to
the complexities of the patient's condition, the Dr trains and monitors Manual Stretching
exercises. Manual stretching exercises were performed using the precor stretching equipment
and targeted to the following body part/body region(s): left lumbar, lumbar and right lumbar
- Advised
- TX Effect: Treatment rendered without incident and responding as expected
- Next Visit: continue with treatment plan as scheduled

clamshells 3x10
ASSESSMENT
Diagnosis
Summary: Caryn Is a 50 yo female who presents with primary c/o B knee and ankle pain since she was In an MVA on 7/30/23.
Her pain Is worse on the right than the left and she reports her low back pain which she had had previously has been worse
since the MVA as well. She reports radiographic findings prior to MVA showed a tumor in her right knee, and she has orders for
another radiograph ordered. Her Initial functional scale score is 26/80 on LEFS and 22/50 on Oswestry, indicating severe
disability. She has pain/difficulty with functional tasks, including prolonged standing/walking, going up/down stairs (worsegoing
down than up), getting up/down froma chair/toilet, performing household chores, and working atthe VA. She currently works 2
days per week after having 2 mos off following MVA and is applying to work 5 daysper week fulltime 4 weeks from now. Pt will
benefit from skilled PT services atthis time to address impairments found and return to PML of function without pain or
limitation. Good prognosis and recovery with skilled PT is expected atthis time.
See code details above
Problems & Goals
1. Goals
1. Type: Long term
Description: Patient will increase LEFS score by15 points in order to demonstrate a significant increase in level
offunction and tolerance to functional tasks such as going up/down stairs, doing dishes, cooking, walking, and
getting up/down from toilet
Target Timeframe: Eight weeks
. Type: Short term
Description: Patient will increase Ankle AROM to 10 degrees of dorsiflexion and 55 degrees of plantarflexion to
be able to perform ADL's with less compensation
Target Timeframe: Four weeks
. Type: Long term
Description: Patient will increase LE strength to a 4/5 or greater in all planes without pain during testing

Attestation signed by Bowers, Rebecca C, MD at 8/23/2023 1:50 PM
| saw and evaluated the patient with the resident/fellow. | discussed the case with the resident/fellow and agree
with the findings and plan as documented.
HPI
Chief Complaint
Patient presents with
+Abscess
The patient is a 50-year-old female presenting transferred from Saint Joe's hospital with impression of a left
peritonsillar abscess. The patient presented to the outside hospital initially with complaints of 2 days of dysphagia,
odynophagia, left-sided neck pain. At the outside hospital day performed a CT neck with contrast for her to use it
neck pain and swelling which demonstrated the PTA. After imaging results theoutside hospital administered 1g
vancomycin, 600 mg clindamycin, Toradol and Dilaudid for pain control, and transferred the patient here for further
management. On presentation here the patient continuesto complain of the left-sided neck pain hisdysphagia and
odynophagia. She reports she has not eaten anything in2 days with the exception of obstacles, has been trying to
drink but this has been difficult to day. She did havea fluid bolus at the outside hospital. The patient otherwise
denies having any nausea, vomiting, fever, chills, difficulty breathing, abdominal pain, or any other complaints at this
time. She has not had any similar symptoms previously.
No data recorded

[DOB-712] - ED in PAV A Emergency Department (continued)
ED Provider Note (continued
Patient seen and examined with Dr. Bowers.
Caryn Leigh McCay is a 50y.o. female with pcDM presenting transferred from OSH with c/o dysphagia, odynophagia,
left sided neck pain, with impression of left PTA from OSH imaging. History was obtained by patient. On arrival, the
patient was hemodynamically stable, non-febrile, and non-toxic appearing. Physical exam was notable for very
uncomfortable appearing patient, tearful, however without any respiratory distress, trismus limits exam of tonsils but
uvula appears deviated to right. Differential diagnosis includes but is not limited to strep throat, viral URI,
retropharyngeal abscess, peritonsillar abscess, laryngitis, Lemierre's. Ruling out the most morbid conditions drove my
assessment.
External notes independently reviewed from outside hospital and pertinent for:
- CT neck with contrast which demonstrated asymmetric enlargement of the left palatine tonsillar area with an
underlying 1.8 cm focus of developing phlegmon/abscess. This displaces the airway to the right and there is mild to
moderate narrowing of the airway
- medications PTA included 1g Vanc, 600mg Clindamycin, Decadron
Initial Assessment:
- 50 yo Fwith hx DM here with left PTA, needing abscess drainage and abx
Interventions/M edications:
- fluid bolus, Dilaudid for pain control
- Promptly consulted and had an interactive discussion with ENT who came to evaluate the patient
- Abscess pocket drained at bedside by ENT
- Per ENT ok for remainder of management outpatient with 7-10 days of antibiotics
- We elected for 10 days of Clindamycin instead of Augmentin given her PCN allergy
Reassessment:
- after abscess drainage the patient feels much more comfortable and her symptoms have largely resolved. She is
eager to bedischarged home now
Impression:
1. Peritonsillar abscess
Disposition:
- discharge home with outpt abx
ED Prescriptions
None

Consult Note
08/18/23
HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Caryn Leigh McCay is a 50 y.o. female with history of diabetes who presented to UK ED for concerns of left
peritonsillar abscess. She reports her symptoms of left throat and neck pain along with odynophagia began 2 days
ago. Her has extreme throat pain requiring multiple doses of IV opiates. She has some URI symptoms including clear
drainage from nose and eyes, sneezing, but denies any trismus, difficulty breathing, fevers, n/v/d, or recent infections.
She has had poor PO intake secondary to odynophagia over the last 2days.
She has history of tonsillectomy in her childhood. She was seen at OSH where she was given vancomycin,
clindamycin, toradol, IV steroids, dilaudid anda fluid bolus. She reports mild symptom improvement with the above
treatments. She has diabetes and takes 50 of Levemer BID per patient. He smokes 1ppd since age 13. She has
history of drug use snorting, no IV, but stopped 6 years ago.
REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
A 14-point review of systems was performed and is negative except as noted in HPI.

w/o masses/lesions/mucosal ulceration. True vocal folds freely mobile without significant glottic gap. There is a
region of polypoid change anteriorly on the right vocal fold, though not concerning in appearance.
Widely patent
Incision and Drainage of Abscess:
DISCUSSION OF PROCEDURE: After informed consent was obtained verbally, the patient was prepped and draped
in the standard fashion for peritonsillar abscess drainage. Topical anesthesia inthe form of benzocaine spray was
used to anesthetize the oropharynx. Next, 1% lidocaine with 1:100,000 epinephrine was injected into the left anterior
tonsillar pillar for added local anesthesia. Next, the oropharynx was palpated and the area of greatest fluctuance was
identified. Using an 18-gauge spinal needle, the area of greatest fluctuance was aspirated. No purulence was
removed. An 11 blade was used to make an incision inthe medial most aspect of the abscess. Purulence was
expressed and the patient reported foul-tasting drainage. No culture was able to be obtained. The abscess pocket was
thoroughly irrigated with saline. The patient tolerated the procedure well. Dr. Fritz was available for this procedure.
LABS
WBC 13.3 at OSH
Strep test negative

ASSESSMENT/PLAN
Caryn Leigh McCay is a 50 y.o. female with histoy of diabetes who presents with concerns of small parapharyngeal
abscess on left. Her symptoms have progressively worsened with mild improvement with antibiotics at OSH. Her
abscess is lateral and extends inferiorly to epiglottis but no edema of glottis. Airway and subglottis widely patent on
scope exam. The abscess pocket was drained at bedside with purulent drainage. Patient reported near immediate
improvement in her symptoms
Recommendations:
- From ENT perspective, OK for discharge and outpatient management
- 7-10 days of PO augmentin
- Recommend against PO steroids given poorly-controlled DM
- Pain medication per ED
- Advance diet as tolerated
- Patient should follow-up with ENT (Dr. Fritz) in 3-4 weeks. We will arrange for this appt.
This Consult, Assessment, and Plan has been discussed with Dr. Frtiz, Attending Physician. Thank you for involving
us inthe care of this patient. Please call with any questions or concerns.
Mit Patel, MD - PGY1

Discharge Instructions from 8/15/2023
Noel, Brennan S, MD at 8/18/2023 1806 from 8/15/2023
You were evaluated in the Emergency Department for an abscess. Please keep the areas surrounding the abscess clean
and dry. Ifthe abscess progresses, you may have to have the abscess incised and drained. If your abscess was incised
and drained during your visit, please follow wound care instructions attached. It is important to be aware of any new or
worsening symptoms, as some problems maytake longer to appear.
Follow-up with ENT as instructed.
¢ Werecommend you take Ibuprofen and/or Tylenol every 6 hours as needed for pain.
e Alternating Tylenol and Ibuprofen every 3 hours is an excellent strategy for reducing fever or pain.
o For instance, at 12 p.m. take Ibuprofen, then at 3 p.m. take Tylenol, then at 6 p.m. take Ibuprofen.
- Please take the prescribed antibiotics for the full course of the medication as directed.
Follow up with your primary care physician within 2 days for awound check.
Return to the Emergency Department if you experience worsening pain, persistent fevers greater than 100.4, an

Thank you for choosing us for your care.
Open Wounds(No packing or drain)
Open wounds should be dressed with dry gauze until fully healed.
You will need to change the dressing every 24 hours.
o Wash hands before and after dressing wound.
Clean area with soap and water in shower.
Vaseline may be applied to wound.
You may soak the wound several times a day in warm, soapy water.
o Replace with a fresh dressing after soaking is completed.
Continue wound care until wound is healed.
Incision and Drainage with Packing
You will need to change the dressing every 24 hours.
o Wash hands before and after dressing wound.
Clean area with soap and water in shower.
Gently pull packing strip out %-1 inch and cut with scissors.
Vaseline may beapplied to wound.
Apply non-stick dressing or pad and tape.
Ifthere is still drainage, you may put gauze over non-stick pad.
o Do not put gauze directly over wound. It will stick to the packing and possibly
pull it out at the next dressing change.
Do this once a day until packing isgone.
Continue wound care after packing is out until wound is healed.
Loop Drainage
You will need to change the dressing every 24 hours.
o Wash hands before and after dressing wound.
Please keep the areas surrounding the abscess clean and dry.
Vaseline may beapplied to wound.
Keep area clean and cover with gauze to absorb the residual drainage.
o Drainage may persist for up to 7-10 days and isacceptable.
° Gently wiggle the loop daily to encourage drainage.
Clean area with soap and water in shower.
You may soak the wound several times a day in warm, soapy water.
o Feplace with a fresh dressing after soaking is completed.
The loop drain can removed at Urgent Care or PCP's office once:
o Drainage has stopped.
o Cellulitis hasimproved.
o Usually is within 7-10 days.
Continue wound care after loop drain is out until wound is healed.

What everyone needs to know about AIDS
Kentucky law requires that we inform you about AIDS.

[DOB-764] - ED in PAV A Emergency Department (continued)
Documents (continued)
HIV stands for “human immunodeficiency virus,“ which isa virus that attacks the body's immune system. HIV makes it harder
for people with ittorecover from other infections and illnesses. AIDS stands for “acquired immune deficiency syndrome,“
which isthe health effects that happen ifHIV is not treated. Withtreatment, a person living with HIV can be healthy and live
nearly as long as a person who does not have HIV. Treatment for HIV also greatly lowers the chance that someone with HIV
will pass ittoothers.
HIV isnot spread through activities such as hugging or shaking hands or sharing a drinking glass. HIV is only spread through
blood, sexual fluids, or breast milk.
This usually happens by:
« Having sex without condoms
« Sharing needles
A mother living with HIV can pass HIV toher baby, but this is now very uncommon in the US as long as the mother knows that
she has HIV and isbeing treated. It is also very rare that HIV ispassed by blood transfusions or transplanted organs, because
all donated blood and organs are now tested for HIV.
HIV is mostly spread by people who do not know they have HIV. This iswhy itis so important to get tested for HIV atleast
once in your lifetime, and atleast every 6 months if you are having sex without condoms or sharing needles.
Treatment with Controlled Substances
Federal and state laws regulate controlled substances (drugs) that may be abused. Kentucky law requires that you consent
totreatment with these drugs before you can receive them. Some illnesses and injuries can result in pain. Some drugs can
make the pain more tolerable. Some other drugs can increase focus and reduce hyperactivity. Use ofthese drugs can cause
nausea, sleepiness, drowsiness, vomiting, constipation, sleeplessness, loss ofappetite, agitation, aggravation of depression,
dry mouth, confusion, slower breathing, and loss of coordination making itunsafe to drive or operate machinery. These drugs
can result in physical dependence, meaning that abrupt stopping may lead to withdrawal symptoms, psychological
dependence, meaning that stopping may cause you focrave the drug, tolerance, meaning you need more drugs toget the
same effect and addiction, meaning you may develop problems based ongenetic or other factors. You must tell your doctor if
you are pregnant or are considering pregnancy.
Living Wills
Please tell your provider if you have a Living Will, Advance Directive, PowerofAttorney or any other document that allows
someone to make healthcare decisions for you ordocuments your wishes regarding your care. You areresponsible for
telling your care team Ifyou have anyofthese kinds of documents.

Documents (continued)
Instructions
‘You were evaluated inthe Emergency Department for an abscess. Please keep the areas surrounding the abscess clean
and dry. Ifthe abscess progresses, you may have to have the abscess incised and drained. Ifyour abscess was incised
and drained during your visit, please follow wound care instructions attached. Itis important tobe aware ofany new or
worsening symptoms, as some problems may take longer toappear.
Follow-up with ENT as instructed.
* We recommend you take Ibuprofen and/or Tylenol every 6 hours as needed for pain.
* Alternating Tylenol and !buprofen every 3 hours is anexcellent strategy for reducing fever or pain.
©For instance, at 12 p.m. take Ibuprofen, then at 3p.m. take Tytenol, then at 6 p.m. take [buprofen.
=Please take the prescribed antibiotics for the full course ofthe medication as directed.
Follow up with your primary care physician within 2 days for a wound check.
Retum tothe Emergency Department ifyou experience worsening pain, persistent fevers greater than 100.4, an increase
inarea of redness, increased tendemess/warmth around the abscess, foul smelling discharge from the abscess, or any
other concerning symptoms.
Thank you for choosing us for your care.
(Open Wounds (No packing or drain)
* Open wounds should bedressed with dry gauze until fully healed.
* You will need to change the dressing every 24 hours.
+Wash hands before and after dressing wound.
* Clean area with soap and water in shower.
+Vaseline may be applied towound.
* You may soak the wound several times a day in warm, soapy water.
* Replace with a fresh dressing after soaking is completed.
«Continue wound care until wound is healed.
Incision and Drainage with Packing
+You will need to change the dressing every 24 hours.
+Wash hands before and after dressing wound.
* Clean area with soap and water in shower.
»Gently pull packing strip out ¥2-1 inch andcut with scissors,
+Vaseline may be applied towound.
«Apply non-stick dressing or pad and tape.
©Ifthere is still drainage, you may putgauze over non-stick pad.
* Do notput gauze directly over wound. Itwill stick tothe packing and possibly pull it
out at the next dressing change.
* Dothis once a day until packing is gone.
+Continue wound care after packing is out until wound is healed.

Drainage
+You will need to change the dressing every 24 hours.
»Wash hands before and after dressing wound.
* Please keep the areas surrounding the abscess clean and dry.
+Vaseline may be applied towound.
+Keep area clean and cover with gauze toabsorb the residual drainage.
* Drainage may persist for up to 7-10 days and is acceptable.
+Gently wiggle the loop daily toencourage drainage.
* Clean area with soap and water in shower.
+You may soak the wound several times a day in warm, soapy water.
+Replace with a fresh dressing after soaking is completed.
« The loop drain can removed at Urgent Care or PCP’s office once:
° Drainage has stopped.
Cellulitis has improved.
¢ Usually is within 7-10 days.
+Continue wound care after loop drain is out until wound is healed.

. Attached Information Abscess, Antibiotic Treatment Only (English)
Abscess (Antibiotic Treatment Only)
An abscess happens when bacteria get trapped under theskin and start togrow. Pus forms inside the abscess as the
‘body reacts to thebacteria. An abscess can happen with an insect bite, ingrown hair, blocked oilgland, pimple, cyst, or
puncture wound, Itis sometimes call a boil.
In the early stages, your wound may be red and sore. For this stage, you may get antibiotics. Iftheabscess doesn't get
better with antibiotics, it will need tobe drained with a small cut.
Home care
These tips will help you care for your abscess at home:
Soak the wound in hot water or apply hot packs (small towel soaked in hot water) to the area for20 minutes at a
time. Do this 3 to4 times a day, oras instructed. Use a new towel each time. Wash the towels afterward because
they may be contaminated with bacteria after use.
Don't cut, squeeze, or pop the boil yourself.
Put antibiotic cream orointment on the skin 3 to4times a day, unless something else was prescribed. Some
ointments include an antibiotic plus a pain reliever.
{fyour healthcare provider prescribed antibiotics, don't stop taking them until you have finished the medicine, or
you are told tostop.
* You may use an over-the-counter pain medicine tocontrol pain, unless another pain medicine was prescribed. Talk
with your provider before taking these medicines if you have chronic liver or kidney disease or ever had a stomach
ulcer ordigestive bleeding.
Follow-up care
Follow up with your healthcare provider, or as advised. Check your wound each dayfor signs that the infection may be
getting worse (see below).
When toget medical advice
Call your healthcare provider right away if any ofthese occur:
* An increase in redness or swelling
* Red streaks in the skin leading away from the abscess
* An increase in local pain or swelling
* Fever of100.4°F (38°C) or higher, or as directed by your provider
* Pus or fluid coming from the abscess
* Boil retums after getting better

Narrative: DISPATCH:
EMSS wasdispatched by Jessamine County £911 to 1250 KEENE RO for reports ofa Female patient
needing transfered to UK ER foradmission/surgery forperi tonsillar abscess.
RESPONSE:
EMSS initiated an non-Immediate response. Responding no lights or sirens to the scene.
ARRIVAL:
Upon arrivalwe Found a 50 year ald female. Patientis Found lying on ER stretcher/bed at emergency
room. Patientis conscious and alert, AQx4 with a GCS of 15.Airway is patent with normal respirations,
radial pulse noted tobe strong, The patient's chief complaint is left side neck pain . Duration ofchief
complaint is2 days. General impression of the patient is moderate distress. The patient was ambulatory
‘onscene and able to Fully move with a good range of motion. The patient was secured to stretcher
19 straps and stretcher i i semiFowlers.
ASSESSMENT:
Assessmentofchief complaint includes: Left side neck pain, left ear pain, throat pain, painful
swallowing. Patient is rating their pain 5 out of 10 on the pain scale.
Upon EMS arrival Nurs¢ Mike Orr advised that patient presented toER this morning with complaints of
left side neck pain, left earpain, and painful swallowing. Nurse Mike Orrdvised thataCT scan revealed
that patient had a peri tonsillar abscess. Nurse Mike Orr advised that patient need tobe transferred to
UK ERFor admission and surgery to drain the abscess. Patient was found lying in bed in ER. Patient was
alert and oriented x4 witha patent airway. Nurse Mike Orr noted that patient’s Vancomycin had finished
funning. Nurse Mike Orr then disconnected the Vancomycin and then started the Clindamycin drip.

HPI
Chief Complaint
Patient presents with
* Motor Vehicle Crash
Room/Bed: 72/72
History provided by: Patient
Language interpreter used: No
Caryn Leigh McCay is a 50 y.o. female with PMH of diabetes who presentstothe emergency department from scene
for evaluation as a trauma due to a MVC. Patient was a(n) restrained driver without ejection. She reports driving at
approximately 45 miles per hour when another car pulled out in front of her, causing her to hit a brick wall. The patient
believes she may have lost consciousness for a brief period during the accident. She describes the impact as forceful,
with airbags deploying.
Currently endorsing pain in chest, cervical, and thoracic spine without associated weakness, numbness, and tingling.
Denies any recent fevers, night sweats, chills, shortness of breath, chest pain, nausea/ vomiting, cough, diarrhea, or
paresthesias.
GCS: (spontaneously (+4), oriented (+5), and obeys commands (+6)) Score = (15)
LOC: (+) LOC
Blood thinners: (-) Blood thinners
Seatbelt sign: (+) Seatbelt sign
Airbags: (+) Airbags deployment
FAST Exam: Negative Fast

Amount and/or Complexity of Data Reviewed

Patient Progress
Patient progress: improved
Caryn Leigh McCay isa 50y.o. female presenting for evaluation of Motor Vehicle Crash.
Differential diagnosis includes but is not limited to subarachnoid hemorrhage, subdural hematoma, epidural
hematoma, intraparenchymal hemorrhage, cervical artery dissection, traumatic brain injury, diffuse axonal injury,
concussion, fracture, muscle strain, tendonitis, muscle sprain, vascular injury, nerve injury
Plan: Trauma labs/scans, pain control, reassess
Labs interpreted by me. Labs show hyperglycemia with a glucose of 436, elevated beta Hydroxybutyrate of 0162, Aic of
13.5%, pH is 7.43 with a lactate of 1.6, negative pregnancy test, trops of 10 and 11 respectively
At this point, it appears that the patient has avoided acute, life-threatening traumatic injuries secondary to the time
that has elapsed since the incident, the patient's well appearance, their essentially normal vital signs, and the nonfocal
nature of repeated physical examinations here in the emergency department. Additionally, the patient's imaging

Alvarez, Jonathan R, MD at 7/31/2023 0407 from 7/28/2023
You have been evaluated in the Emergency Department today following an injury or accident. Your evaluation did not
show evidence of medical conditions requiring emergent intervention at this time. It is important to be aware of any
new or worsening symptoms, as some problems may take longer to appear. Additionally, it iscommon for
musculoskeletal pain to become more intense before it begins to improve.
e¢ We recommend you take Ibuprofen and/or Tylenol every 6 hours as needed for pain.
e Alternating Tylenol and Ibuprofen every 3 hours is an excellent strategy for reducing fever or pain.
o Forinstance, at 12 p.m. take Ibuprofen, then at 3 p.m. take Tylenol, then at 6 p.m. take Ibuprofen.
Please follow up with your primary care physician in 2-3 days.
Return to the ER immediately for worsening or uncontrolled pain, difficulty walking, numbness or weakness in
your arms or legs, chest pain, shortness of breath, confusion, vomiting, or for any other concerning symptoms.
Thank you for choosing usfor your care.

Resulting lab; IMAGING
Narrative:
CLINICAL INDICATION:
poly trauma
TECHNIQUE:
Imaging ofthe chest abdomen and pelvis was performed, from thoracic inlet through pubic symphysis, using spiral technique,
following administration of IV contrast, Omnipaque 350, 100 mL according tothe CTA thoracic aorta/chest and CTA
Abdomen/Pelvis protocol. Reformatted images in the coronal, sagittal, and oblique planes were generated from the axial data
set tofacilitate diagnostic accuracy. In addition, 3D images were created and reviewed.
Multiple axial CT images were obtained through level of pelvis per CT Bony Pelvis protocol. The axial CT data setwas used to
generate high resolution reformatted images in the coronal and sagittal planes to facilitate diagnostic accuracy and treatment
Printed on 10/11/23 9:33 AM Page 134

Contrast
Atelectasis
Cortical Destruction
Sclerotic Ribs
CT Of Pelvic Bones
McCay, Caryn Leigh (MRN 006386932) Page 135
[DOB-923] - ED in PAV A Emergency Department (continued)
Imaging (continued)
planning.
Total DLP (Dose-Length Product): 4958 mGy-cm. Please note: The reported value represents the total ofone or more individual
components during the CT acquisition onthis date and atthis time, and as such, the same value may appear in more than one
CT report depending on the interpreting/reporting physicians.
COMPARISON:
CTA chest abdomen pelvis 4/16/2016
FINDINGS:
Chest:
Lower neck: The supraglottic soft tissues appear circumferentially thickened.
Aorta/Vessels: No acute thoracic aortic pathology. No periaortic hematoma. No filling defect within the pulmonary arteries to
suggest pulmonary embolism.
Pleural/Pericardial Space: No pneumothorax. No pleural effusions. No pericardial effusion.
Lymph Nodes: No lymphadenopathy within the chest.
Lungs: Except for minimal dependent atelectasis, the lungs are clear.
Mediastinum: Otherwise unremarkable.
Chest wall: No chest wall hematoma or contusion.
Bones: No acute fracture within the chest. Sclerotic changes and cortical destruction ofthe left posterior ribs 3through 8, new
from 2016.
Abdomen:
Vessels: The abdominal aorta and its major branches are unremarkable.
Liver/Gallbladder/Biliary System: The liver demonstrates homogeneous enhancement. Cholecystectomy No intra- or extrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation.
Spleen: The spleen enhances homogeneously.
Pancreas: The pancreas enhances homogeneously.
Adrenals: The adrenals are morphologically unremarkable.
Kidneys: The kidneys demonstrate symmetric nephrogram and excretion. No renal or ureteral calculi. No hydronephrosis.
Bowel/Mesentery: The small bowel loops are not dilated. The large bowel loops are not dilated. The appendix is visualized and
normal.
Lymph Nodes: No lymphadenopathy within the abdomen or pelvis.
Fluid Survey: No free fluid in the abdomen. No free fluid in the pelvis.
Pelvis: The pelvic viscera are unremarkable.
Body Wall: Normal.
Bones: No acute fracture within the abdomen or pelvis.
CT bony pelvis no pelvic fracture.
Impression:
1. No acute vascular pathology within the chest abdomen or pelvis.
2, No acute findings in the chest, abdomen, or pelvis.
3. The left posterior ribs 3-9, are indistinct possibly motion artifact.

Narrative:
CLINICAL INDICATION:
poly trauma
TECHNIQUE:
Imaging ofthe chest abdomen and pelvis was performed, from thoracic inlet through pubic symphysis, using spiral technique,
following administration of IV contrast, Omnipaque 350, 100 mL according tothe CTA thoracic aorta/chest and CTA
Abdomen/Pelvis protocol. Reformatted images in the coronal, sagittal, and oblique planes were generated from the axial data
set tofacilitate diagnostic accuracy. In addition, 3D images were created and reviewed.
Multiple axial CT images were obtained through level of pelvis per CT Bony Pelvis protocol. The axial CT data setwas used to
generate high resolution reformatted images in the coronal and sagittal planes to facilitate diagnostic accuracy and treatment
planning.
Total DLP (Dose-Length Product): 4958 mGy-cm. Please note: The reported value represents the total of one or more individual
components during the CT acquisition on this date and atthis time, and as such, the same value may appear in more than one
CT report depending on the interpreting/reporting physicians.
COMPARISON:
CTAchest abdomen pelvis 4/16/2016
FINDINGS:
Chest:
Lower neck: The supraglottic soft tissues appear circumferentially thickened.
Aorta/Vessels: No acute thoracic aortic pathology. No periaortic hematoma. No filling defect within the pulmonary arteries to
suggest pulmonary embolism.
Pleural/Pericardial Space: No pneumothorax. No pleural effusions. No pericardial effusion.
Lymph Nodes: No lymphadenopathy within the chest.

Kidneys: The kidneys demonstrate symmetric nephrogram and excretion. No renal or ureteral calculi. No hydronephrosis.

Imaging ofthe entire cervical, thoracic, and lumbar spine was performed, using spiral technique, without contrast administration.
Reformatted images in the coronal and sagittal planes were generated from the axial data setto facilitate diagnostic accuracy
and/or surgical planning.
Total DLP (Dose-Length Product): 4958 mGy*cm. Please note: The reported value represents the total of one or more individual
components during the CT acquisition on this date and atthis time, and as such, the same value may appear in more than one
CT report depending on the interpreting/reporting physicians.
COMPARISON:
CT CT L-spine 4/16/2016.
FINDINGS:
Cervical Spine:
Vertebrae: No acute fracture. Hypertrophic and degenerative changes. Airwithin the posterior inferior aspect of C5.
Uncovertebral joint hypertrophy most notable atC5-6. Anterior osteophyte at C5-Cé6.
Alignment: Normal spinal alignment.
Paraspinal Soft Tissues: No paraspinal hematoma.
Lung Apices: No pneumothorax atthe lung apices.
Thoracic Spine:
Vertebrae: No acute fracture. Mild multilevel degenerative change without significant bony neuroforaminal or spinal canal
stenosis. Multilevel moderate degenerative facet arthritis in the thoracic spine. Mild predominantly anterior disc osteophytic
ridging. Chronic Schmor''s nodes. Minimal subtle degenerative listhesis in the upper thoracic spine.

Lumbar Spine:
Vertebrae: No acute fracture. Multilevel degenerative changes, worst atthe left L5-S1 facet without significant bony
neuroforaminal or spinal canal stenosis. Osteophyte adjacent the left L4-L5 facet could cause narrowing of the spinal canal.
Alignment: Normal spinal alignment.

Imaging (continued)
Reformatted images in the coronal and sagittal planes were generated from the axial data set to facilitate diagnostic accuracy
and/or surgical planning.
Total DLP (Dose-Length Product): 4958 mGy*cm. Please note: The reported value represents the total of one or more individual
components during the CT acquisition onthis date and atthis time, and as such, the same value may appear in more than one
CT report depending onthe interpreting/reporting physicians.
COMPARISON:
CT CT L-spine 4/16/2016.
FINDINGS:
Cervical Spine:
Vertebrae: No acute fracture. Hypertrophic and degenerative changes. Air within the posterior inferior aspect of C5.
Uncovertebral joint hypertrophy most notable at C5-6. Anterior osteophyte at C5-C6.
Alignment: Normal spinal alignment.
Paraspinal Soft Tissues: No paraspinal hematoma.
Lung Apices: No pneumothorax atthe lung apices.
Thoracic Spine:
Vertebrae: No acute fracture. Mild multilevel degenerative change without significant bony neuroforaminal or spinal canal
stenosis. Multilevel moderate degenerative facet arthritis in the thoracic spine. Mild predominantly anterior disc osteophytic
tidging. Chronic Schmorl's nodes. Minimal subtle degenerative listhesis in the upper thoracic spine.
Alignment: Normal spinal alignment.
Paraspinal Soft Tissues: No paraspinal hematoma. Small focus ofair posterior tothe left T2 lamina.
Lumbar Spine:
Vertebrae: No acute fracture. Multilevel degenerative changes, worst atthe left L5-S1 facet without significant bony
neuroforaminal or spinal canal stenosis. Osteophyte adjacent the left L4-L5 facet could cause narrowing of the spinal canal.
Alignment: Normal spinal alignment.
Paraspinal Soft Tissues: No paraspinal hematoma.
Impression:
1. No acute fracture or malalignment ofthe cervical spine.
2. No acute fracture or malalignment ofthe thoracic spine.
3. No acute fracture or malalignment ofthe lumbar spine.
CRITICAL RESULT:
No.
COMMUNICATION:
Per this written report.

CT Bony Pelvis Resulted: 07/31/23 0045, Result status: Final result
Ordering provider: Tyagi, Aaron C, MD 07/30/23 2302 Order status: Completed

Turner, David G, MD
Van Wyk, Charl S, MD
Performed: 07/30/23 2319 - 07/30/23 2337 Accession number: 15555561
Resulting lab; IMAGING
Narrative:
CLINICAL INDICATION:
poly trauma
TECHNIQUE:
Imaging ofthe chest abdomen and pelvis was performed, from thoracic inlet through pubic symphysis, using spiral technique,
following administration of IV contrast, Omnipaque 350, 100 mL according tothe CTA thoracic aorta/chest and CTA
Abdomen/Pelvis protocol. Reformatted images in the coronal, sagittal, and oblique planes were generated from the axial data
set tofacilitate diagnostic accuracy. In addition, 3D images were created and reviewed.
Multiple axial CT images were obtained through level of pelvis per CT Bony Pelvis protocol. The axial CT data setwas used to
generate high resolution reformatted images in the coronal and sagittal planes to facilitate diagnostic accuracy and treatment
planning.
Total DLP (Dose-Length Product): 4958 mGy-cm, Please note: The reported value represents the total of one or more individual
components during the CT acquisition on this date and atthis time, and as such, the same value may appear in more than one
CT report depending on the interpreting/reporting physicians.
COMPARISON:
CTAchest abdomen pelvis 4/16/2016
FINDINGS:
Chest:
Lower neck: The supraglottic soft tissues appear circumferentially thickened.
Aorta/Vessels: No acute thoracic aortic pathology. No periaortic hematoma. No filling defect within the pulmonary arteries to
suggest pulmonary embolism.
Pleural/Pericardial Space: No pneumothorax. No pleural effusions. No pericardial effusion.
Printed on 10/11/23 9:33 AM Page 153

This document contains private information – any unlawful disclosure is prohibited
2023.07.30 Caryn McCay UK Hospital records.pdf - Page 88
Atelectasis
Sclerotic Ribs
Cortical Destruction
CT Of Pelvic Bones
McCay, Caryn Leigh (MRN 006386932) Page 154
[DOB-945] - ED in PAV A Emergency Department (continued)
Imaging (continued)
Lymph Nodes: No lymphadenopathy within the chest.
Lungs: Except for minimal dependent atelectasis, the lungs are clear.
Mediastinum: Otherwise unremarkable.
Chest wall: No chest wall hematoma or contusion.
Bones: No acute fracture within the chest. Sclerotic changes and cortical destruction ofthe left posterior ribs 3through 8, new
from 2016.
Abdomen:
Vessels: The abdominal aorta and its major branches are unremarkable.
Liver/Gallbladder/Biliary System: The liver demonstrates homogeneous enhancement. Cholecystectomy No intra- or extrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation.
Spleen: The spleen enhances homogeneously.
Pancreas: The pancreas enhances homogeneously.
Adrenals: The adrenals are morphologically unremarkable.
Kidneys: The kidneys demonstrate symmetric nephrogram and excretion. No renal or ureteral calculi. No hydronephrosis.
Bowel/Mesentery: The small bowel loops are not dilated. The large bowel loops are not dilated. The appendix is visualized and
normal.
Lymph Nodes: No lymphadenopathy within the abdomen or pelvis.
Fluid Survey: No free fluid in the abdomen. No free fluid in the pelvis.
Pelvis: The pelvic viscera are unremarkable.
Body Wall: Normal.
Bones: No acute fracture within the abdomen or pelvis.
CT bony pelvis no pelvic fracture.
Impression:
1. No acute vascular pathology within the chest abdomen or pelvis.
2. No acute findings in the chest, abdomen, or pelvis.
3. The left posterior ribs 3-9, are indistinct possibly motion artifact.
4, Circumferential thickening ofthe supraglottic soft tissues. Possibly motion artifact. Fullness posterior tongue base possibly
lingual tonsils.
5. No pelvic fracture.
CRITICAL RESULT:
No.
COMMUNICATION:
Per this written report.

Mediastinum: Otherwise unremarkable.
Chest wall: No chest wall hematoma or contusion.
Bones: No acute fracture within the chest. Sclerotic changes and cortical destruction of the left posterior ribs 3 through 8, new
from 2016.
Abdomen:
Vessels: The abdominal aorta and its major branches are unremarkable.
Liver/Gallbladder/Biliary System: The liver demonstrates homogeneous enhancement. Cholecystectomy No intra- or extrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation.
Spleen: The spleen enhances homogeneously.
Pancreas: The pancreas enhances homogeneously.
Adrenals: The adrenals are morphologically unremarkable.

Kidneys: The kidneys demonstrate symmetric nephrogram and excretion. No renal or ureteral calculi. No hydronephrosis.
Bowel/Mesentery: The small bowel loops are not dilated. The large bowel loops are not dilated. The appendix is visualized and
normal.
Lymph Nodes: No lymphadenopathy within the abdomen or pelvis.
Fluid Survey: No free fluid in the abdomen. No free fluid in the pelvis.
Pelvis: The pelvic viscera are unremarkable.
Body Wall: Normal.
Bones: No acute fracture within the abdomen or pelvis.
Impression:
1. No acute vascular pathology within the chest abdomen or pelvis.
2. No acute findings in the chest, abdomen, or pelvis.
3. The left posterior ribs 3-9, there are cortical destructive changes and underlying sclerotic changes which were not present in
2016. Correlate for any history of radiation therapy or trauma. Malignancy is less likely given the similar location offindings
across multiple rib levels.
4.Circumferential thickening ofthe supraglottic soft tissues.
CRITICAL RESULT:
No.

Admin instructions: If patient NPO, and/or lacks IV access, and POC BG is less than or equal
to70 mg/dL, give 1mg glucagon IM.
Recheck POC BG 15 minutes after administration and retreat if necessary
until POC BG is > 100.
Do not give in patient with pheochromocytoma or insulinoma.
For IV push or IM use: reconstitute vial with 1mL sterile water for
injection.

What everyone needs toknow about AIDS ‘
Kentucky law requires that we Inform you about AIDS.
HIV stands for "numan immunodeficiency virus,” which is avirus that attacks the body's immune systam. HIV makes it
harder for people with it to recover from other infections and ilinesses. AIDS stands for "acquired immune deficiency
syndrome,” which isthehealth effects that happen if HIV is not treated, With treatment,aperson living with HIV can be
healthy and live nearly as long as aperson who does not have HIV. Treatment for HIV also greatly lowers the chance that
someone with HIV will pass it to others.
HIV is not spread eg activities such as hugging or shaking hands or sharing adrinking glass. HIV Is only spread
through blood, sexual fluids orbreast milk.
This usually happens by: * Having sex without condoms. * Sharing needles

Additional Information
Preventing Falls in the Home
As you get older, falls are more likely. That's because your reaction time slows. Your muscles andjoints may also get stiffer,
making them less flexible. Iliness, medications, and vision changes can also affect your balance. A fall could leave you
unable tolive on your own. To make your home safer, follow these tips:
Moving:
* Getup slowly after you sit or lie down.
+Wear shoes inside andoutside the house. Do not go barefoot or wear slippers.
Floors:
* Put nonskid pads under area rugs.
+Remove throw rugs. Or hold the rugs in place with 2-sided tape or nonslip backing.
+Replace worn floor coverings.
« Tack carpets firmly toeach step on carpeted stairs. Put nonskid strips on the edges ofuncarpeted stairs.
* Keep floors and stairs free ofclutter and cords.
* Arrange furniture so there are clear pathways,
* Clean up any spills right away.
Stairs:
« Always use the handrails. There should be handrails on both sides. The handrails should be as tong as the stairs,
+Use acontrasting color on the top edge ofall steps. This will help you see the steps better,

Bathrooms:
Install grab bars in the tub or shower and next tothe toilet,

Lighting:
+Keep aflashlight in each room.
+Put a nightlight along the pathway between the bedroom and the bathroom. Some nightlights come on on their
own after dark.
« Place a lamp close tothe bed where itis easy toreach.

Why do |need a naloxone prescription?
Naloxone is a rescue medicine. Itis only used totreat an opioid overdose. Most people who are prescribed an opioid do
not have any problems. But evenif you are careful, there is still a risk ofaccidental overdose.
Make sure someoneclose teyou knows the signs of overdose and how to give naloxone.
Most opioid deaths are accidents
Opioid overdose deaths are happening more often in the U.S. In fact, aperson dies from opioid overdose every 11
minutes,
* In 2017, opioid overdose caused more than 47,000 deaths.
+Most ofthose deaths were accidents.
+Nearly 4out of10 people who die from opioid overdose have an opioid prescription.
Death from overdose ismore common than you think.

Attached Information MVA, No Serious Injury (English)
Motor Vehicle Accident: No Serious Injury
‘You ofyour child have been seen today because ofa car accident. Your exam does not show any sign ofserious injury
from yourcar accident. It's important towatch for any new symptoms that might beasign ofhidden injury.
Itcan be normal tofeel sore and tight inyour muscles and back the next day, and not just the muscles you initially injured.
Remember, all the parts ofyour body are connected, so while initially one area hurts, the next day another may hurt.
Injuries cause inflammation, which then causes the muscles totighten up and hurt more. After the initial worsening, it
should slowly improve over the next few days. However, report more severe pain toyour healthcare provider.
Even without a definite head injury, you can still get aconcussion from your head suddenly jerking forward, backward, or
‘sideways. It's common tohave a mild headache and feel tired, nauseated, or dizzy. Concussions and even bleeding can still
‘occur, especially if you've had arecent injury, take blood thinners, or are aver age 65. Know the waming signs that you
should report toyour healthcare provider.
Even without physical injury, acar accident can be very stressful. Itcan cause emotional or mental symptoms after the
event. These may include:
* General sense ofanxiety and fear
* Recurring thoughts or nightmares about the accident
« Trouble sleeping or changes in appetite
* Feeling depressed, sad, or low inenergy
* Being irritable or easily upset
+Feeling theneed toavoid activities, places, or people that remind you ofthe accident
In most cases, these are normal reactions. Andthey're not severe enough tointerfere with your normal activities. They
should go away in a fewdays orafew weeks. Talk with your healthcare provider ifthese reactions last longer, getworse, or
disrupt your daily life.
Home care
Muscle pain, sprains and strains
Evenif you have no visible injury, it's common to besore all over, and have new aches and pains the first couple ofdays
after an accident. Take iteasy at first, and don't overdo it.
* Atfirst, don't try to stretch out the sore spots. If there is a strain, stretching may make it worse.
* You can use an ice pack or cold compress on the sore spots for up to20 minutes at a time, as often as you feel
comfortable. This may help reduce the inflammation, swelling, and pain. To make an ice pack, put ice cubes in a
plastic bag that seals at the top. Wrap the bag in a thin towel or cloth. Don't put the ice pack directly on theskin.
+Sometimes, after the pain and inflammation heal you can be left with a good amount ofstiffness. In this case, you
can use a heating pad, especially on your low back,
Wound care
* Ifyou have any scrapes or abrasions, they often heal in about10 days. It's important to keep the abrasions clean while
they first start toheal. Follow wound care instructions from your healthcare provider. Watch for early signs of
infection such as:
* Increasing redness, warmth, or swelling around the wound
+Fever
+Red streaking lines around the wound
Caryn L. McCay (MRN: 006386932) «Printed at 7/31/2023 4:10 AM Page 100f11 pic
Printed on 10/11/23 9:34 AM Page 203

* Draining pus
Medicines
* Talk toyour healthcare provider before taking new medicine, especially ifyou have other medical problems or are
taking other medicines.
* Ifyou need anything forpain, you can take acetaminophen or ibuprofen, unless you were given a different pain
medicine touse. Ibuprofen is an anti-inflammatory agent and helps more with muscle soreness, Talk with your
provider before using these medicines ifyou have medicine allergies, chronic liver or kidney disease, stomach uker
orgastrointestinal bleeding, or are taking blood thinner medicines. Always follow your provider's instructions.
Be careful if you're given prescription pain medicines, narcotics, or medicines for muscle spasm. They can make you
sleepy, dizzy and can affect your coordination, reflexes andjudgment. Don’t drive or do work where you can injure
yourself when taking them.
Follow-up care
Follow up with your healthcare provider, or as advised, Ifemotional or mental symptoms persist or getworse, follow up
with your provider right away. You may have a more serious traumatic stress reaction. There are treatments that can help.
IfX-rays or a CT scan were done, you'll betold if there is a change that affects treatment.
Call 917
Call 911 if any ofthese occur:
* Trouble breathing
* One pupil is larger than the other
* Repeated vomiting
Headache that worsens and does not go away
Restlessness or agitation
+ Confusion, drowsiness, or trouble arousing
+ Fainting, loss ofconsciousness, convulsions, or seizures
+ Rapid heart rate
+ Trouble with speech or sight
* Trouble walking, toss ofbalance, numbness orweakness in 1side ofyour body, facial droop
When toget medical advice
Call your healthcare provider right away ifany ofthe following occur:
+ New or worsening pain in neck, back, belly, arm, orleg
* Redness, swelling, or pus coming from any wound
« Mental and emotional symptoms that don't get better or that get worse

Operative Description
Indications:
Ms. Caryn Leigh McCay is a 49 y.o. female evaluated for surgical management of anal squamous cell
carcinoma. Identified at time condyloma excision. Predominantly noted to be in situ. Ulcerated lesion noted
within the anterior anorectal canal. Scheduled for excision. All risks, benefits, goals, and alternatives were
discussed with Ms. Caryn Leigh McCay. Patient agreed to above stated procedures.
Findings:
Scarred lesion along the anterior perineum extending to the anal canal. Full-thickness excision performed.
Operative Procedure (Narrative):
1. SCIP INF-1: Prophylactic antibiotics received within 1-hour prior to surgical incision: Clindamycin
900mg IV.
2. SCIP INF-10: Perioperative temperature control: YES.
3. SCIP VTE-2: Appropriate VTE prophylaxis: Bilateral SCDs (only).
Patient was properly identified and consent was obtained. Patient was brought to the operating room.
Transferred to the operating room table. GETA was administered. Patient was placed in the prone position.
Perineum was prepped and draped in standard surgical fashion. Visual inspection demonstrated scarred
area in the anterior midline extending up to the anal opening from prior excision.
Digital rectal examination performed. No masses or lesions palpated. Sawyer retractor was placed within
the anorectal canal. All 4 quadrants were inspected. Anterior midline scarring was noted consistent with
previously visualized ulcerated perianal skin extending into the anal canal. The anorectal mucosa appeared
healthy. Full-thickness excision was performed measuring approximately 6 x 2 cm. Elliptical incision was
made extending up to the anal opening. Full-thickness excision was performed including skin subcutaneous
tissue and a small rim rectal wall muscle. Defect was closed in multiple layers. Bleeding points controlled.
No direct evidence of residual squamous cell carcinoma.
Patient tolerated the procedure well. Patient was transferred to the post anesthetic care unit without surgical
or anesthetic complication.
PLAN AS FOLLOWS:
