Care Plan (continued)
Goal: Patient-Specific Goal (Individualized)
Dates:
Disciplines:
Start: 07/20/22 Expected End: 07/24/22
Nurse, Interdisciplinary
Goal: Absence of Hospital-Acquired Illness or Injury
Dates:
Disciplines:
Expected End: 07/24/22
Nurse, Interdisciplinary
Intervention: Identify and Manage Fall Risk
Frequency:
Description:
Per CPG
Perform standard risk assessment on admission using a validated tool or comprehensive
approach appropriate tothe patient; reassess fall risk frequently, with change in status or transfer
to another level of care.
Communicate fall injury risk to interprofessional healthcare team.
Determine need for increased observation, equipment and environmental modification, such as
lowbed, signage and supportive, nonskid footwear.
Adjust safety measures to individual developmental age, stage and identified risk factors.
Reinforce the importance ofsafety and physical activity with patient and family.
Perform regular intentional rounding to assess need for position change, pain assessment and
personal needs, including assistance with toileting.
Intervention: Prevent Skin Injury
Frequency:
Description:
Per CPG
Perform a screening for skin injury risk, such as pressure or moisture associated skin damage on
admission and at regular intervals throughout hospital stay.
Keep all areas of skin (especially folds) clean and dry.
Maintain adequate skin hydration.
Relieve and redistribute pressure and protect bony prominences; implement measures based on
patient-specific risk factors.
Match turning and repositioning schedule toclinical condition.
Encourage weight shift frequently; assist with reposition ifunable to complete independently.
Float heels off bed; avoid pressure on the Achilles tendon.
Keep skin free from extended contact with medical devices.
Encourage functional activity and mobility, as early as tolerated.
Use aids (e.g., slide boards, mechanical lift) during transfer.
Intervention: Prevent and Manage VTE (Venous Thromboembolism) Risk
Frequency:
Description:
Per CPG
Assess for VTE (venous thromboembolism) risk.
Encourage and assist with early ambulation.
Initiate and maintain compression or other therapy, as indicated, based onidentified risk in
accordance with organizational protocol and provider order.
Encourage both active and passive leg exercises while in bed, if unable toambulate.
Intervention: Prevent Infection
Frequency:
Description:
Per CPG
Maintain skin and mucous membrane integrity; promote hand, oral and pulmonary hygiene.
Optimize fluid balance, nutrition, sleep and glycemic control to maximize infection resistance.
Identify potential sources of infection early to prevent or mitigate progression ofinfection (e.g.,
wound, lines, devices).
Evaluate ongoing need for invasive devices; remove promptly when no longer indicated.
Goal: Optimal Comfort and Wellbeing
Dates:
Disciplines:
Expected End: 07/24/22
Nurse, Interdisciplinary
Intervention: Monitor Pain and Promote Comfort

Care Plan (continued)
Frequency: Per CPG
Description: Assess pain level, treatment efficacy and patient response at regular intervals using aconsistent
pain scale.
Consider the presence and impact of preexisting chronic pain.
Encourage patient and caregiver involvement in pain assessment, interventions and safety
measures.
Intervention: Provide Person-Centered Care
Frequency: Per CPG
Description: Use a family-focused approach to care.
Develop trust and rapport by proactively providing information, encouraging questions, addressing
concerns and offering reassurance.
Acknowledge emotional response to hospitalization.
Recognize and utilize personal coping strategies.
Honor spiritual and cultural preferences.
Goal: Readiness for Transition of Care
Dates: Expected End: 07/24/22
Disciplines: Nurse, Interdisciplinary
Intervention: Mutually Develop Transitlon Plan
Frequency: Per CPG
Description: Identify available resources for support (e.g., family, friends, community).
Identify and address barriers to ongoing treatment and home management (e.g., environmental,
financial).
Provide opportunities to practice self-management skills.
Assess and monitor emotional readiness for transition.
Establish or reconnect linkage with outpatient providers or community-based services.

What everyone needs to know about AIDS
Kentucky law requires that we inform you about AIDS.

Documents (continued
HIV stands for “human immunodeficiency virus,“ which isa virus that attacks the body's immune system. HIV makes it harder
for people with ittorecover from other infections and illnesses. AIDS stands for “acquired immune deficiency syndrome,“
which isthe health effects that happen ifHIV is not treated. Withtreatment, a person living with HIV can be healthy and live
nearly as long as a person who does not have HIV. Treatment for HIV also greatly lowers the chance that someone with HIV
will pass ittoothers.
HIV isnot spread through activities such as hugging or shaking hands or sharing a drinking glass. HIV is only spread through
blood, sexual fluids, or breast milk.
This usually happens by:
« Having sex without condoms
« Sharing needles
A mother living with HIV can pass HIV toher baby, but this is now very uncommon in the US as long as the mother knows that
she has HIV and isbeing treated. It is also very rare that HIV ispassed by blood transfusions or transplanted organs, because
all donated blood and organs are now tested for HIV.
HIV is mostly spread by people who do not know they have HIV. This iswhy itis so important to get tested for HIV atleast
once in your lifetime, and atleast every 6 months if you are having sex without condoms or sharing needles.
Treatment with Controlled Substances
Federal and state laws regulate controlled substances (drugs) that may be abused. Kentucky law requires that you consent
totreatment with these drugs before you can receive them. Some illnesses and injuries can result in pain. Some drugs can
make the pain more tolerable. Some otherdrugs can increase focus and reduce hyperactivity. Use ofthese drugs can cause
nausea, sleepiness, drowsiness, vomiting, constipation, sleeplessness, loss ofappetite, agitation, aggravation of depression,
dry mouth, confusion, slower breathing, and loss of coordination making itunsafe to drive or operate machinery. These drugs
can result in physical dependence, meaning that abrupt stopping may lead to withdrawal symptoms, psychological
dependence, meaning that stopping may cause you tocrave the drug, tolerance, meaning you need more drugs toget the
same effect and addiction, meaning you may develop problems based ongenetic or other factors. You must tell your doctor if
you are pregnant or are considering pregnancy.
Living Wills
Please tell your provider if you have a Living Will, Advance Directive, PowerofAttorney or any other document that allows
someone to make healthcare decisions for you ordocuments your wishes regarding your care. You areresponsible for
telling your care team Ifyou have anyofthese kinds of documents.

the chest. Lungs: Except for min imal dependent atelectasis, the lungs are clear. Chest Wall: No chest wall
hematoma or contusion. Bones: No acute fracture within the chest. ABDOMEN: Liver/Gallbladder/Biliary
system: The liver demonstrates homogeneous enhancement. C holecystectamy. There is intrahepatic and

homogeneously. Pancreas: The pancreas enhances hamageneously. Adrenals: The adrenals are morphol
ogically unremarkable. Kidneys: The kidneys demonstrate symmetric nephrogram and excretion. No renal
or ureteral calculi. No hydronephrosis. Bowel/Mesentery: The small bowel loops are not dilated. The large
bowel loops are not dilated. The appendix is visualized and normal. Vessels/Lymph nodes: The abdominal
aorta is unremarkable. No lymphadenopathy within the abdomen or pelvis. Fluid survey: No free fluid in the
abdomen. No free fluid in the pelvis. Pelvis: Small am ount of fluid within the endometrial canal. Multiple
small nabothian cysts in the cervix. Body wall: Fat-containing umbilical hernia. Bones: Comminuted fracture
of the coccyx. Patchy sclerosis of bilateral sacral ala. IMPRESSION: 1. No acute traumatic aortic injury. 2.
No acute findings in the chest. 3. Comminuted fracture of the coccyx. 4. Patchy sclerosis of the bilateral
sacral ala is nonspecific but can be seen with psoriasis or Reiter's syndrome. 5. Small amount of fluid within

REQUESTING PHYSICIAN: WALTER LUBBERS REASON FOR EXAMINATION/PROCEDURE: RAD
PDP:Y * MVC EXAMINATION / PROCEDURE: CTA THORACIC AORTA Apr 16 2016 - 23:26 CT
ABDOMEN/PELVIS W CONTRAST Apr 16 2016 -23:26 *** PRELIMINARY RESULT *** PRELIMINARY
RESULT *** PRELIMINARY RESULT *** CLINICAL INDICATION: MVC. TECHNIQUE: Imaging of the chest
abdomen and pelvis was performed, from thoracic inlet through pubic symphysis, using spiral technique,
following administration of IV cont rast, Omnipaque 300, 97 mL according to the CTA thoracic aorta and CT
Abdomer/Pelvis protocol. Delayed (excretory phase) images were performed through the kidneys.
Reformatted images in the coronal, sagittal, and oblique planes were generated from the axial data set to
facilitate diagnostic accuracy. In addition, 3D images were created and reviewed. TOTAL DLP (DoseLength Product): 3475. Please note: The reported value represents the total of one or more individual
components during the CT acquisition on this date and at this time, and as such, the same value may
appear in more than one CT report depending on the interpreting/reporting physicians. COMPARISON:
None. FINDINGS: Mild motion artifact limits ev aluation. CHEST: Aaria/vessels: No acute traumatic aortic
injury. No periaortic hematoma. Pleural/pericardial space: Ne pneumothorax. No pleural effusions. No
pericardial effusion. Lymph Nodes: No lymphadenopathy within the chest. Lungs: Except for minimal
dependent atelectasis, the lungs are clear. Chest Wail: No chest wall hematoma or contusion. Bones: No
acute fracture within the chest. ABDOMEN: Liver/Gallbladder/Biliary system: The liver demonstrates homo Prelim
geneous enhancement. Cholecystectomy. There is intrahepatic and extrahepatic ductal dilatation. The
common duct measures up 10 10 mm. Spleen: The spleen enhances homogeneously. Pancreas: The
pancreas enhances homogeneously. Adrenals: The adrenals are morphologically unremarkable. Kidneys:
‘The kidneys demonstrate symmetric nephrogram and excretion. No renal or ureteral calculi. No
hydronephrosis. Bowel/Mesentery: The small bowel loops are not dilated. The large bowel loops are not
dilated. The appendix is visualized and normal. Vessels/Lymph nodes: The abdominal aorta is
unremarkable. No lymphadenopathy within the abdomen or pelvis. Fluid survey: No free fluid in the
abdomen. No free fiuid in the pe Ivis. Pelvis: Small amount af fluid within the endometrial canal. Body wall:
Fat-containing umbilical hemia. Bones: No acute fracture within the abdomen or pelvis. Patchy sclerosis of
bilateral sacral ala. IMPRESSION: 1. No acute traumatic aortic injury. 2. No acute findings in the chest. 3.
No acute traumatic findings in the abdomen or pelvis. 4. Patchy sclerosis of the bilateral sacral ala is
nonspecific but can be seen with psoriasis or Reiter's syndrome. 5. Small amount of fluid within the
endometrial canal may be physiologic. 6. Status post cholecystectomy with mild intra and extra hepatic
ductal dilatation which may be due to postcholecystectomy state. CRITICAL RESULT: No.
COMMUNICATION: Per this written report. ATTESTATION: Not applicable. Transcribed by: PSCB Apr 17
2016 12:12A Dictated by: SCOTT D. BERL, M.D. on Apr 17 2016 12:12A

No pneumothorax. No pleural effusions. No pericardial effusion. Lymph Nodes: No lymphadenopathy within
the chest. Lungs: Except for min imal dependent atelectasis, the lungs are clear. Chest Wall: No chest wall
hematoma or contusion. Bones: No acute fracture within the chest. ABDOMEN: Liver/Gallbladder/Biliary
system: The liver demonstrates homogeneous enhancement. C holecystectamy. There is intrahepatic and
extrahepatic ductal dilatation. The common duct measures up to 10 mm. Spleen: The spleen enhances
homogeneously. Pancreas: The pancreas enhances hamageneously. Adrenals: The adrenals are morphol
ogically unremarkable. Kidneys: The kidneys demonstrate symmetric nephrogram and excretion. No renal
or ureteral calculi. No hydronephrosis. Bowel/Mesentery: The small bowel loops are not dilated. The large
bowel loops are not dilated. The appendix is visualized and normal. Vessels/Lymph nodes: The abdominal
aorta is unremarkable. No lymphadenopathy within the abdomen or pelvis. Fluid survey: No free fluid in the
abdomen. No free fluid in the pelvis. Pelvis: Small am ount of fluid within the endometrial canal. Multiple
small nabothian cysts in the cervix. Body wall: Fat-containing umbilical hernia. Bones: Comminuted fracture
of the coccyx. Patchy sclerosis of bilateral sacral ala. IMPRESSION: 1. No acute traumatic aortic injury. 2.
No acute findings in the chest. 3. Comminuted fracture of the coccyx. 4. Patchy sclerosis of the bilateral
sacral ala is nonspecific but can be seen with psoriasis or Reiter's syndrome. 5. Small amount of fluid within
the endometrial canal may be physiologic. Multiple small nabothian cysts in the cervix. 6. Status post
cholecystectomy with mild intra and extra hepatic ductal dilatation which may be due to
postcholecystectomy state. CRITICAL RESULT: Ne. COMMUNICATION: Per this written report.

“ADULT" Post Foley Removal Algorithm:
(1)Prompt patient to spontaneously vaid within 6 hours
(2) Itspontaneously void greater than or equal to 400 in 6 hours -> assess
for signs of bladder distention, pain or any troubling symptoms -> if
present, SCAN BLADDER
(3) Ifspontaneous void less than 400 in 6 hours, SCAN BLADDER
Adult Post Fal (4) If no void in 6 hours -> SCAN BLADDER . Discontinue
932 Removal Pr otneal (5) If bladder scan is fess than 400, ensure adequate hydration and rescan [DOB-3002] 00:01 dvia
Instructions in 2 hours -> contact service and communicate results of bladder scan; if . Patient
" bladder scanvolume is greater than or equal to 400, IN and OUT cath, Discharge
contact service, andcommunicate results; Continue l/Ocath every 6 hours
if nospontaneous void (Target: 400 ml output/cath); ifgreater than 400 per
cath on more than one occasion, consider increasing cath frequency to
every 4 hours and every 2
hours as needed, ifafter 48 hours, still no void or inadequate spontaneous
void, notify team, and consider urology consult

ADULT" Post Foley Removal Algorithm:
patient to spontaneously void within 6 hours
If spontaneously void greater than or equal to 400 in 6 hours -> assess for signs of bladder distention, pain or any troubling
-> ifpresent, SCAN BLADDER
spontaneous void less than 400 in 6 hours, SCAN BLADDER
lf no void in 6 hours -> SCAN BLADDER
If bladder scan is less than 400, ensure adequate hydration and rescan in 2 hours -> contact service and communicate
of bladder scan; ifbladder scan volume is greater than or equal to400, INand OUTcath, contact service, and
results; Continue 1/0 cath every 6 hours ifno spontaneous void (Target: 400 ml output/cath); if greater than 400
cath on more than one occasion, consider increasing cath frequency to every 4 hours and every2
as needed; ifafter 48hours, still no void or inadequate spontaneous void, notify team, andconsider uralogy consult

If a range dose is ordered, unless specific criteria are designated, the
range order shall be interpreted to give the lowest dose with the remainder
given for unrelieved symptoms and no adverse reactions. The higher dose
may be given for extreme symptoms or ifthe patient has previously shown

ED General Note “[Charted Location: PCF-Emergency Department] [Date of Service: 23 May 17 22:20] [Authored: 2017-05-23 22:20] for Visit:
006386932-7143, Final, Not Revised, Signed in Full, General
Evaluation Time:
+Patient seen on: 23-May-2017 22:20
Evaluation:
CHIEFCOMPLAINT: medical evaluation
HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: MCCAY, CARYN LEIGH is a44y female that presents for amedical evaluation. Pt reports that she
was arrested ataa store for shoplifting while high on heroin and crack about 4 days ago. Atthe time, police believed that she was
attempting tohide drugs by concealing them inside of her body, so she was brought here forevaluation. Xray of abdomen and
vaginalrectal searches yielded only normal results. Today, they found her cocaine and fentanyl levels tabe elevated from that original
day, so they sent her here tohave a CT ofher abdomen. Denies fever, abdominal pain, or N/V/D. She states that she did not attempt
toconceal any drugs.
PAST MEDICAL HISTORY: DM, nephrolithiasis
PAST SURGICAL HISTORY: C:section, cholecystectomy, lithotripsy
ALLERGIES: Reviewed per nursing records.
SOCIAL HISTORY: Negative for Current tobacco, alcoho, or recreational drug use.
FAMILY MEDICAL HISTORY: Reviewed with patient and not pertinent
REVIEW OF SYSTEMS: All systems were reviewed with the patient and negative except as noted in the HPI.
VITALS: VITALS (last 24h) [retrieved for MCCAY, CARYN LEIGH at23May 2017 22:20):
Tc: 36.9 Tmax: 36.9@ 23 May 20:37
Tf: 98.4 Tmax: 98.4 @ 23 May 20:37
HR: 119 (119 - 119)
BP: 165/105 (165/105 - 165/105)
RR: 18 (18- 18) |SpO2: 96% (96%- 96%)
PHYSICAL EXAM:
GENERAL: Sitting up in bed, nontoxic. Well-developed, well-nourished, noacute distress
HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. PERRL. Mucous membranes moist
HEART: Regular rhythm, no murmurs, gallops, or rubs appreciated
LUNGS: Clear bilaterally with normal effort and good airmovement. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi
ABDOMEN: Soff, nontender, nondistended. Bowel sounds present
NEURO: Alert, awake, and oriented to person, place, and time.
LABS/IMAGING:
CT Abdomen/pelvis without contrast
IMPRESSION:
4. No acute abnormality.
2.No definite foreign body or material.
MEDICAL DECISION MAKING: Patient was seen and examined with Dr. Ghali. Patient is a44-year-old female presents to UKfrom jail
for concem ofpossible rectal/vaginal foreign body. FMC was requesting a CT scan. CT scan was performed which did not show any
foreign body. Patient and no other symptoms and was discharged back to jail.
IMPRESSION: concern for foreing body
DISPOSITION: de
Scribe Attestation: |, Dr. lan Boyd, have seen this patient with the scribe (Rory Young) and agree with his documentation as above.
The ED Course/ MDM is accurate as documented by myself.

Consult Follow Up Note *-GME - Medicine / General Medicine [Charted Location: PCF ED - OBS 210- A] [Date af Service: 21 Apr 16 11:08] (Authored:
2016-04-21 11:08] for Visit: 006386932-6107, Final, Revised, Signed inFull, General
ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:
43y female with DM and multiple sexual risk factors, came in after MVC with non operative fractures, complaining ofvaginal itching.
1. MVC
- Right tibial plateau fracture, coccyx fracture, left enthesophyte fracture ofpatella
- Non Operative management per ortho
-PT/OT
2. Vaginal Itching/Discharge
- Normal external genitalia, non tender cervix, milky odorless discharge noted.
-Likely candidal vaginitis
~S/P Fluconazole 150mg Once
-UAbland, Gonorhea PCR, and Chlamydia PCR are pending
~Will need outpatient follow up with OBGYN
3. Rectal imitation
-Visible protrusion in perineal area
~Does not appearto be a hemorthoid
~Given history of HPV, concem for anogenital war
~Would recommend quick outpatient OBGYN ‘alow for possible excisional biopsy and treatment
4.History ofHPV
~Per Per Patient positive forHPV in the past
LastPAP Smear was over 7years ago
- Friable appearing cervix
- Quick outpatient GYN Appointment for PAP Smear and further risk stratification
5.DM
-AIC of89
- Sliding scale insulin
- Given sugar control inpatient, may benefit from some scheduled insulin
Will sign off, please call back with questions.
Charlie Bodine PGY2
330-1771

ChiefComplaint:
Consult for rectal irritation and vaginal itching
History of Present Illness:
43y year old female with ahistory ofanxiety and diabetes who presented after an MVC with butt pain, right knee pain, left knee pain.
Patient wasa front passenger in avehicle traveling down Broadway in Lexington when acar pulled out in front ofthem and they struck
theside. Restrained, no LOC, airbags deployed. Patient complains of rear end pain, right leg pain and left patella pain. She was
admitted tothe ortho service with fractures ofthe right tibial plateau fracture, coccyx fracture, left enthesophyte fracture ofpatella
All fractures were managed non operatively. She is aplacement issue currently.
Medicine was consulted because ofvaginal itching/irritation with some discharge, as well as perirectal discomfort, feeling like
somethingis protruding from her rectum. She denies any history of ids, nochronic On further i
patient has a history ofmultiple sexual partners, as well as bouts ofanal sex. She states that she has a history ofHPV, but has not
had good follow up and has not had aPAP Smear in over 7years. She denies any history of STI's but has had multiple bouts of
vaginal candidiasis in the past, and that is what this feels like.

Header Text
Tobacco Treatment Specialist Note [Charted Location: PCF ED - OBS 210 - A] [Date of Service: 19 Apr 16 11:23] [Authored: 2016-04-19 11:23] for
Visit: 006386932-6107, Complete, Entered, Signed in Full, General
MCCAY, CARYN LEIGH is a 43y female who is seen today for tobacco cessation counseling.
Reports smoking 1 PPD prior to admission. Was provided a nicotine patch in the ED but
this was not continued for unclear reasons (ordered as one time medication?). She is
currently experiencing nicotine withdrawal symptoms since patch effect has “worn off"
and requesting this be continued. Has quit previously using nicotine patches and feels
they are very helpful. Has Humana Caresource, which will cover patches with a prescription
at discharge.
Recommendation: consider re-starting nicotine replacement patch 21 mg/day. Please
provide patient with a prescription for nicotine replacement patches at discharge: 21
mg/day #28 followed by 14 mg/day #28 followed by 7 mg/day #28.

Darville APRN, AudreyK(Nurse Practitioner) (Signed on 19-Apr-16 11:23)

PLAN
. Type: Long term
Description: Patient will increase LEFS score by15 points In order to demonstrate a significant increase in level
offunction and tolerance to functional tasks such as going up/down stairs, doing dishes, cooking, walking, and
getting up/down from toilet
Target Timeframe: Eight weeks
. Type: Short term
Description: Patient will Increase Ankle AROM to 10 degrees of dorsiflexion and 55 degrees of plantarflexion to
be able to perform ADL's with less compensation
Target Timeframe: Four weeks
. Type: Long term
Description: Patient will Increase LE strength to a 4/5 or greater In all planes without pain during testing
Target Timeframe: Eight weeks
. Type: Long term
Description: Patient will report pain In the knees or ankles no greater than a 3/10 in with walking 30 minutes,
carrying 25# box 20 feet, performing 3x10 sit to stands, and cleaning her kitchen
Target Timeframe: Eight weeks
. Type: Short term
Description: Pt will demonstrate increased hip IR PROM to 40 deg or greater bilaterally without pain
Target Timeframe: Four weeks
. Type: Long term
Description: Pt wil be able to perform 20 step-ups on6inch step without pain greater than 2/10 and with good
form
Target Timeframe: Eight weeks

Frequency: Three perweek

Standardized Tests
Lower Extremity Functional Scale
Description: The Lower Extremity Functional Scale is used to evaluate the impairment ofa patient with lower extremity
musculoskeletal condition or disorders.
Please rate your pain level with activity. (9)
1: Any of your usual work, housework, or school activities. Moderate difficulty. (2)
2:Your usual hobbies, recreational, or sporting activities. Moderate difficulty. (2)
3: Getting into or out ofthe bath. Moderate difficulty. (2)
4:Walking between rooms.Alittle bit of difficulty. (3)
5: Putting on your shoes or socks. Moderate difficulty. (2)
6: Squatting. Quite a bit of difficulty. (1)
7: Lifting anobject, like a bag of groceries from the floor. Moderate difficulty. (2)
8: Performing light activities around your home. Moderate difficulty. (2)
9: Performing heavy activities around your home. Quite a bit of difficulty. (1)
10: Getting into or out of a car. Moderate difficulty. (2)
11: Walking 2 blocks. Moderate difficulty. (2)
12: Walking a mile. Quite a bit of difficulty. (1)
13: Going up or down 10 stairs (about 1flight ofstairs).Quite a bit of difficulty. (1)
14: Standing for1hour. Extreme difficulty or unable to perform activity. (0)
15: Sitting for 1hour. Extreme difficulty or unable to perform activity. (0)
16: Running on even ground. Extreme difficulty or unable to perform activity. (0)
17: Running on uneven ground. Extreme difficulty or unable to perform activity. (0)
18: Making sharp turns while running fast. Extreme difficulty or unable to perform activity. (0)
19: Hopping. Extreme difficulty or unable to perform activity. (0)
20: Rolling over in bed. A little bitof difficulty. (3)
Total Score: 26 /80
Additional Comments: 67.5% disability
Outcomes Disability/Symptom Score 26/80

The Oswestry Disability Index Is an extremely Importanttool that researchers and disabllity evaluators useto measure a patient's
functional disability.

{Amothe ving with HIV can pass HIV to her baby, but this Is now very uncommon in theUS as long as the mother knows
that she has HIV and is being treated. ItIs also very rare that HIV Ispassed by blood transfusionsor transplanted organs, because alldonated blood and organs are now tested for HIV.
HIV is mostly spread by people who do not know they have HIV. This is why itis 80 important to get tested for HIV atleast
‘once inyour lifetime, and at least every 6months if you arehaving sex without condoms orsharing needles.
‘Treatment with Controlled Substances:
Federal and state laws regulate controlled substances (drugs) that may be abused. Kentucky lawrequires that you consent
totreatment with these drugs before you can receive them. Some illnesses and injuries can result inpain. Some drugs can
make the pain more tolerable. Some other drugs can increase focus and reduce hyperactivity. Use ofthese drugs can
cause nausea, sleepiness, drowsiness, vomiting, constipation, sleeplessness, loss of appetite, agitation, aggravation of
depression, dry mouth, confusion, slower breathing, and loss of coordination making it unsafe todrive or operate
machinery. These roge can result in physical dependence, meaning thet abrupt stopping may lead to withdrawal
symptoms, nce, Meaning that stopping may cause you to crave the drug, tolerance, meaning you
reed more orientatesame effect and addiction, meaning you may davelop problems based on genetic orother
factors. You must tell your doctor ifyou are pregnant orare considering pregnancy.
Living Wills
Please tell your provider ifyou have aLiving Wil, Advance Directive, PowerofAttorney orany other document that allows
someone to make healthcare decisions foryou ordocuments your wishes regarding your care. You are responsible
for telling your care team if you have any of these kinds of documents.

JONSENT FOR ANESTHESIA AND MONITORING
FOR YOUR PROCEDURE (Patient Label Here)
To the patient: This consent form shows that we have. discussed the anesthesia cere we will give you during your
planned procedure.
(- ( us CA Me Saanesthat an anesthesia provider will
monitor your body functions afd give you anesthesia.
Types and risks of anesthesia
There are some health risks with anesthesia. These are in addition to the risks of the procedure itself. As shown below, we
have discussed with you:
1. The type of anesthesia planned for your procedure, and
2. The risks that come with it, and
3. Alternatives, if available, to the type of anesthesia.
he risks listed here do not include every possible risk. Unexpected risks or complications may happen.
Monitored anesthesla care (also called “MAC") ormonitored sedation
eet What should |expect? You will get anesthesia medicine through an IV. This will calm you, make you
iscussed) less aware of the procedure, and help any medicines injected to numb the operated area work belter,
Fecepied You may remember hearing voices or having mild discomfort.
(patient What are the risks? They include, but are not limited to, allergic reaction to drugs, obstruction of
initials) breathing, and nausea and vomiting after theprocedure.
Regional anesthesia
(check if |What should |expect? This is a medicine that is injected to numb a specific area of the body
discussed) |Common ways todo this are epidural, spinal, or nerve blocks. As part of regional anesthesia, you may
also get medicines to relax. Sometimes, regional anesthesia does not do enough to relieve pain. When
that happens, you may get general anesthesia or a pain medicine through an IV. We can also place a
‘Accepted catheter inyour back, arm or leg before surgery to better control your pain
@ {patient |what are the risks? They include, but are not limited to, low blood pressure, itching or allergic
initials) | reaction to drugs, obstruction or stopping ofbreathing, severe headache, temporary paralysis, nerve
injury, infection ormeningitis.
J General anesthesia
(check if What should |expect? This medicine makes you sleep and not feel pain. You maybreathe in the
issed) _|thedicine or have it injected. Once you are asleep, we will likely place a breathing tube into your
windpipe and remove it before you are fully awake. Thereis a chance that the breathing tube will stay in
place a {ittle longer until you are strong enough to breathe without help. It is common to have nausea or
vomiting, but this can often be treated with medicine. Some patients fear waking up during surgery, but
Accepted this is very rare.
(patient
initials) What are the risks? These include but are not limited to, damage to lips or teeth, sore throat,
headache, eye injury or blindness, infection, transfusion reactions (including excessive bleeding and
kidney damage), drug reactions (including rash, shock, and cardiac or respiratory arrest). blood clots,
lung infection, loss of feeting or limb function, paralysis, stroke or brain injury, hear! failure, heart attack,
and death.
ypes and risks of invasive monitoring
In addition to carefully monitoring your vital signs, we may also use special invasive monitoring. This will depend on the
type of procedure. tf you will have invasive monitoring, we have discussed the type you will have and the risks that come
with it.

Signing Provider Medical Evaluation
The patient presents for a medical evaluation. She was arrested for shoplifting while
high on heroin and crack, and the police believed that she was attempting to hide
drugs by concealing them inside of her body, so she was brought here for an
evaluation. She underwent an abdomen x-ray, vaginal and rectal searches, and lab
tests, which showed elevated cocaine and fentanyl levels from the original day. Also,
today they found her cocaine and fentanyl levels to be elevated from that original
day, so they sent her here to have a CT of her abdomen. The patient underwent a CT
of the abdomen which did not show any foreign body or material.

Performing Provider Evaluation and Care for Left PeritonsillarAbscess
The patient presents with an abscess. She was transferred from the outside hospital
with complaints of 2 days of dysphagia, odynophagia, and left-sided neck pain. The
patient had a CT neck with contrast which demonstrated an underlying 1.8 cm focus
of developing phlegmon/abscess that displaced the airway to the right and there
was mild to moderate narrowing of the airway. The patient had a peritonsillar
abscess and was seen and examined by Dr. Bowers. The abscess was drained at
bedside by ENT and she is doing well now.

04.16.2024 Performing Provider Follow Up for Diabetic Neuropathy
The patient presents for a follow-up evaluation of her diabetic neuropathy. She has
been smoking cigarettes for 37.00 years and has a history of car accident in 2016.
She was on Lyrica for neuropathic pain from her spine, but she had to stop due to
the sedative component. The patient reports that she fell within the last week and
was given Tramadol and Flexeril for which she is almost out, but reports that they
have helped her back pain. She has a history of poor healing gluteal, foot wounds.
The patient will continue with medication therapy and will return to the clinic in 1
month to reassess response to the change in medications.

04.16.2024 Performing Provider Follow Up for Diabetic Neuropathy
The patient presents for a follow-up evaluation of her diabetic neuropathy. She has
been smoking cigarettes for 37.00 years and has a history of car accident in 2016.
She was on Lyrica for neuropathic pain from her spine, but she switched to
Gabapentin and experienced a sedative component. The patient reports that she fell
within the last week and was given Tramadol and Flexeril for back pain, which she is
almost out of. She has bilateral diabetic foot neuropathy that has worsened over the
past year affecting her bilateral feet. The patient will increase her Pregabalin to 75
mg BID and return to the clinic in 1 month to reassess response to the changed
medications.
Go To Source Document

mild femorotibial compartment osteoarthrosis with soft tissue swelling, moderate

patient's tibia and fibular fractures are also seen to be healing. In addition, the study

summary, the patient has a closed fracture of the unspecified patella. In addition,
the study showed degenerative changes in the left knee without acute abnormality.

Signing Provider Follow Up on Patient's request for Dilaudid
The patient presents with pain. She is complaining and is asking for Dilaudid. The
patient's compartments are soft and compressible. The healthcare provider
discussed with the patient that prior use of heroine and suboxone will make pain
control a challenge. The patient states that the last time she had surgery, she
required a PICC line for IV ABX and for Dilaudid. The healthcare provider explained to
the patient that she will not be able to be on IV pain medications and that she needs
to have good pain control on PO narcotics.

04.17.2016 Signing Provider Follow Up for Right Tibial plateau fracture
The patient presents for an orthopaedic compartment check. The patient is a 43-
year-old female with a right tibial plateau fracture and a fracture of the coccyx. The
patient's pain is controlled, and there were no acute events. The patient's sensation
is intact to light touch superficial peroneal, saphenous, and sural nerve distributions.
The patient has paresthesias in the deep peroneal and tibial nerves unchanged since
last exam. The patient's AROM was 5/5 in the right lower extremity with no pain
with passive stretch. Additionally, the patient had mild distress, alert and oriented
x3, regular rate and rhythm, non-labored breathing, symmetric chest wall excursion,
non-tender abdomen, and well perfused toes with a capillary refill less than 2

for a CT of the abdomen and pelvis without contrast to rule out a hernia recurrence.

07.30.2023 
22:26:00
Performing Provider Evaluation for Motor Vehicle Crash
The patient presents to the emergency department from the scene for evaluation as
a trauma due to a motor vehicle crash. She was a restrained driver without ejection,
and she reports driving at approximately 45mph when another car pulled out in
front of her, causing her to hit a brick wall. The patient believes she may have lost
consciousness for a brief period during the accident. She is currently experiencing
pain in her chest, cervical, and thoracic spine without associated weakness,
numbness, and tingling. She underwent lab tests, which showed hyperglycemia with
elevated beta hydroxybutyrate of 0.62, A1c of 13.5 %, and abnormal aPTT of 21. She
underwent imaging that revealed no acute findings or traumatic injuries. The patient
is in stable condition and amenable to discharge at this time.
Go To Source Document

Date Role Summary
03.19.2024 Performing Provider Encounter for Neuropathic Pain
The patient presents for neuropathic pain. She has a history of smoking cigarettes
and has been exposed to tobacco smoke. She was on Lyrica for neuropathic pain
from her spine following a car wreck in 2016, and she reports that it helped her. She
fell within the last week and was given Tramadol and Flexeril for her back pain, which
she is almost out of. She has a diagnosis of chronic worsening neuropathy,
uncontrolled diabetes mellitus type 2, severe facet arthropathy, and hypertrophy in
the lumbar spine. The patient was prescribed Gabapentin for neuropathic pain but
has been struggling with the sedation. Therefore, they switched to Lyrica today. She
will return to the clinic in 1 month to reassess her response to the change in
medications.
Go To Source Document
03.19.2024 Performing Provider Encounter for Neuropathic Pain
The patient presents for neuropathic pain. She has a history of smoking cigarettes
and has been exposed to tobacco smoke. She was on Lyrica for neuropathic pain
from her spine, but she is currently taking Gabapentin. She fell within the last week
and was given Tramadol and Flexeril for her back pain. The patient will continue
Gabapentin and will also start taking Lyrica. She will return to the clinic in 1 month to
reassess her response to the change in medications.

03.12.2024 
11:50:00
Signing Provider MRI of the Right Knee with and without Contrast
The patient presents for an MRI of the right knee with and without contrast. The
study shows a complex tear involving the medial meniscus, moderate
chondromalacia within the medial compartment, mild-to-moderate chondromalacia
within the lateral compartment with areas of full-thickness cartilage loss and a
suspected internal osteophyte formation, there is also an irregularity with
fragmentation of the lateral tibial plateau which is presumably a sequela of prior
comminuted depressed tibial plateau fracture, there is mild cortical irregularity along
the articular surface of the lateral tibial plateau, there is a very low T1 and T2 signal
lesion within the lateral epiphysis and metaphysis of the lateral tibia, there is
ganglion cyst formation noted at the gastrocnemius muscle attachments, there are
severe arthritic changes in the patellofemoral compartment with complete loss of
articular cartilage seen along the lateral facet with extensive subchondral and/or
osteochondral-type defects, there is osteophytosis, there is a small suprapatellar
joint effusion. The patient was referred to radiographic correlation.
Go To Source Document
02.05.2024 
16:52:00
Performing Provider Caryn Leigh Mccay's Multiple Abnormal Uptake of the Knees
The patient underwent a nuclear medicine limited bone scan due to right knee pain.
The study shows small field of view imaging of the knees with bilateral uptake. The
left knee has a focal area of uptake along the medial tibial plateau and a larger area
along the lateral femoral condyle. The right knee has more diffuse uptake seen about
the knee articulations both centrally along the peripheries. There is also diffuse
involvement of the patella. The study shows multifocal abnormal uptake about the
knees bilaterally, with worse right sided asymmetry.
Go To Source Document

body in an unknown location. She underwent pelvic and rectal exams, as well as

Ordering Provider Emergency Department Visit due to an Abscess
08.18.2023 Ordering Provider Emergency Department Visit due to Throat Pain
The patient presents with throat pain. She is diagnosed with a peritonsillar abscess.
The patient underwent a CT scan, which showed an asymmetric enlargement of the
left palatine tonsillar area with an underlying 1.8 cm focus of developing phlegmon/
abscess. This displaced the airway to the right and there was mild to moderate
narrowing of the airway. There was edema and probable fluid that extended
inferiorly in the posterior pharynx, effacing the left piriform sinus. The patient is
strep negative, and her labs are today are negative for diabetes mellitus with a
glucose level of 325. The patient is prescribed 1 gram of Vancomycin and 600 mg of
Clindamycin.

04.16.2016 Signing Provider Caryn L. McCay's Discharge Summary
The patient was involved in a vehicle accident and was admitted to the hospital with
a fracture of the coccyx and a tibial plateau fracture of the right leg. The patient was
placed in a knee immobilizer and instructed to use a walker. She also had associated
pain in the right knee, left knee, and patella. She was prescribed AcetaminophenOxycodone, Alprazolam, Cefdinir, Cyclobenzaprine, Enoxaparin, and Gabapentin.

Date Role Summary
08.18.2023 Performing Provider Emergency Department Visit for Small ParapharyngealAbscess on Left
The patient presents to the ED with concerns of a left peritonsillar abscess. She
reports symptoms of left throat and neck pain along with odynophagia that began 2
days ago. She has a history of diabetes, anxiety disorder, fracture of coccyx, and
endocrine, nutritional and metabolic disease. The patient underwent flexible
fiberoptic laryngoscopy which revealed an area of polypoid change anteriorly on the
right vocal fold, but not concerning in appearance. The patient was diagnosed with a
small right sided abscess that was lateral and extended inferiorly to the epiglottis.
The abscess was drained at the bedside with purulent drainage and she reported
near immediate improvement in her symptoms. The patient was prescribed
Augmentin for 7-10 days and referred for follow-up in 3-4 weeks.
Go To Source Document

withdrawal symptoms since the patch effect has "worn off". The patient has quit
previously using nicotine patches and feels they are very helpful. She has Humana

for nicotine replacement patches is recommended.

12.12.2023 Performing Provider Evaluation for Physical Therapy
The patient presents to skilled physical therapy for evaluation with primary concern
of bilateral ankle and knee pain which have been present since she was in an MVA
on 7/30/23. She was t-boned and her knees hit the dash forcefully. She reports her
pain is worse R > L, and rates her current pain a 6/10, pain at worst 10/10 in last 1-2
weeks, and pain at best 5/10. She has difficulty getting up / down from the floor as
well. The patient is working 2 days a week at the VA after 2 months off work and
plans to start full - time in 4 weeks. She reports she has to lay down when she gets
home from work and sometimes while on breaks at work due to pain. Her goal with
physical therapy is to reduce symptoms in order to return to prior level of function
and work full - time at the VA.
Go To Source Document
12.12.2023 Performing Provider Follow-Up on Multiple Orthopedics Conditions
The patient presents for a chiropractic therapy appointment. The patient has a
history of MVA on 7/30/23. She was diagnosed with cervical quad syndrome,
lumbosacral disorders, right trapezius pain, left trapezius pain, right levator scapula
pain, left levator scapula pain, right sternocleidomastoid pain, left
sternocleidomastoid pain, right longus capitis pain, left longus capitis pain, right
lumbar spine enthesopathy, left lumbar spine enthesopathy, right cervical spine
spondylolysis, and left cervical spine spondylolysis. She is referred to physical
therapy.

Go To Source Document
11.21.2023 Performing Provider Follow-Up for Multiple Orthopedic Conditions
The patient presents for a therapeutic exercises appointment. She is here to
continue her physical therapy and was referred to a pain specialist and a
chiropractor. She has a history of hernia surgery in 2016, MVA in 2016 leading to
multiple LE fractures, tumor in R knee diagnosed prior to MVA not thought to be
cancerous, diabethic neuropathy, rectal cancer, and DM II. The patient is having
difficulty with going up and down stairs, as well as difficulty with walking between
rooms, getting into or out of the bath, squatting, lifting an object like a bag of
groceries from the floor, and performing heavy activities around her home. The
patient will continue her physical therapy treatment.
Go To Source Document
11.21.2023 Performing Provider Chiropractic Therapy Visit
The patient presents for a chiropractic therapy appointment. She has a history of
pain in her neck, back, hips, and knees. The patient will increase her hip IR PROM to
40 deg or greater bilaterally without pain.
Go To Source Document
11.21.2023 Performing Provider Follow Up on Femoral Acetabular Impingement
The patient presents for a chiropractic visit. She has a history of pain in the neck,
back, hip, and knee. The patient describes the discomfort as aching, tightness, and
stiffness. Since her last visit she reports feeling the same. Discomfort increases with
movement, applied pressure, sitting, and standing. Discomfort decreases with rest,
chiropractic care, and medication. She experiences discomfort frequently. Severity of
discomfort is described as moderate. Her typical or average pain is rated 7 out of 10.
She is able to perform job duties with some difficulty. The following activities are
limited due to the condition: lifting, bending, standing, walking, sitting, pulling,
pushing, twisting, climbing stairs, sleeping/driving and exercising.
Go To Source Document
11.16.2023 Performing Provider Cervicobrachial Therapy Visit
The patient presents for a therapy appointment. The patient has a history of pain in
the right ankle, shoulder, and neck. The patient has been diagnosed with segmental
and somatic dysfunction of the cervical spine, spondylolysis of the cervical region,
injury of nerve root of cervical spine, and muscle spasms of the back. The patient will
increase ankle AROM to 10 degrees of dorsiflexion and 55 degrees of plantarflexion
to be able to perform ADL's with less compensation.
Go To Source Document
11.16.2023 Performing Provider Cervicobrachial Therapy
The patient presents for a therapy appointment. The patient has a history of pain in
the right ankle, shoulder, and neck. The patient has been diagnosed with segmental
and somatic dysfunction of the cervical spine, spondylolysis of the cervical region,
injury of the nerve root of the cervical spine, and muscle spasms of the back. The
patient will increase ankle AROM to 10 degrees of dorsiflexion and 55 degrees of
plantarflexion to be able to perform ADL's with less compensation.
Go To Source Document
11.16.2023 Performing Provider Follow-Up Visit for Physical Therapy
The patient presents for a continue physical therapy. The patient has pain in the right
ankle and left knee. The patient was given a hot pack, TENS unit, and instructed to
use the medical tape. The patient will continue with the treatment.

12.14.2023 Performing Provider Follow-Up for Multiple Conditions
The patient presents for a physical therapy initial evaluation. She has a history of an
MVA and has been having pain in her neck, back, hips, and knees. She was diagnosed
with cervical spondylolysis, lumbar spondylolysis, right sacroiliac joint dysfunction,
and left medial hamstring myofascial release. She was prescribed manual therapy
exercises, therapeutic exercises, and electrical stimulation.

The patient presents for a chiropractic therapy visit. She has been experiencing
discomfort in the neck, which she describes as tightness and stiffness. The patient is
able to perform her job duties with some difficulty. The following activities are
limited due to the condition: lifting, bending, standing, walking, sitting, sleeping,
driving, and exercising. The patient has been diagnosed with segmental somatic
dysfunction of the cervical spine, spondylolysis of the cervical spine region, and
multiple spinal enthesopathy at various sites in the spine. She will increase her
physical therapy exercises and will follow up in 8 weeks for a re-evaluation.

elbow, right elbow, left knee, right ankle, and left foot. She also has a diagnosis of

pain is rated as mild to moderate. She is able to perform her job with some difficulty
and the following activities are limited due to the condition: standing, walking,

continue with manual therapy exercises and will follow up with the provider in 8
weeks.

11.09.2023 Performing Provider Follow Up on Multiple Orthopedics Conditions
The patient presents for a continue physical therapy from previous day. The patient
received treatment of cervical spondylolysis, right sided lumbosacral and root
disorders, right sided spinal pain enthesopathy, right sided chronic stress ankle
disorder, and left sided lumbosacral and root disorders. The patient tolerated the
addition of R tibiofemoral distraction with passive IR and flexion today, as well as
prone knee flexion stretch with towel gaping. Patient was able to tolerate all
therapeutic exercises, activities, and neuromuscular re-education activities well
today and required only minimal cuesing.

11.09.2023 Performing Provider Chiropractic Therapy Visit
The patient presents for a chiropractic therapy appointment. The patient has
tenderness in the upper to mid-cervical, thoracic, and upper lumbar spine. The
patient's postural analysis shows left head translation, anterior head translation, left
thoracic translation, anterior thoracic translation, right high shoulder, left pelvic
translation, anterior pelvic translation, and left high hip. The patient has muscle
spasms in the right trapezius (upper), left trapezius (upper), left levator scapula, right
levator scapulae, left supraspinatus, right supraspinatus, left sternocleidomastoid,
right sternocleidomastoid, left scaleneus anticus (right), left scaleneus medius (right),
left scaleneus posticus (right), longus capitis (left and right). The patient's range of
motion concern is the entire lumbar spine. The patient also has spinal restrictions in
the atlas, C4, C7, T3, T7 sacrum and L1/L2. Additionally the patient has extraspinal
restrictions in the left elbow and knee and the right shoulder and foot.

Date Role Summary
Not Found Signing Provider Evaluation of a 43-year-old female who presented to the ED as a restrained 
passenger of a vehicle that was struck on the side.
The patient presents to the ED as a restrained passenger of a vehicle that was struck
on the side. She has a right tibial plateau and fibular neck fracture and a coccyx
fracture. The patient was diagnosed with decreased albumin and increased blood
glucose levels. Her last BM was on 4/16/16. She is overweight with a BMI of 29.8,
and her weight is 86.4 kilograms. She is unable to take Penicillin, Erythromycin, and
Codeine, as well as shellfish. She is prescribed Percocet, Xanax, Glucagen, and
insulin, as well as a consistent carbohydrate diet.

Performing Provider Caryn L. Mccay's Visit
The patient presents for a follow-up visit. She underwent a leg X-ray, and the results
showed a fracture in her tibia and fibula. The patient was instructed to use crutches
for assistance with walking and was prescribed Aspirin-81 mg.
Go To Source Document
Not Found Signing Provider Occupational Therapy Discharge Summary
The patient was involved in a motor vehicle collision on 4/16 and was diagnosed
with a right tibial plateau fracture, left patellar fracture, and comminuted coccyx
fracture. The patient was treated with occupational therapy and is able to partially
achieve established goals. The patient is discharged and will be transferred to an
inpatient rehab facility.
Go To Source Document
Not Found Signing Provider Occupational Therapy Evaluation
The patient presents for an occupational therapy initial evaluation. The patient was
admitted on 4/16 s/p MVC and has a history of anxiety, diabetes, C kidney stone, left
leg infection MRS with debridements. The patient has a right tibial plateau fracture,
left patellar fracture and comminuted coccyx fracture. The patient's pain rating at
rest is 10/10 and the patient refused mobility due to pain. The patient's bilateral
upper extremity range of motion was within normal limits, as was the left lower
extremity range of motion. However, the right lower extremity range of motion was
not tested due to pain. The patient's bilateral upper extremity strength was within
normal limits but the left lower extremity strength was within functional limits and
the right lower extremity strength was not tested due to pain.
Go To Source Document
Haeberlin, Jill

Not Found Signing Provider Nutrition Evaluation
The patient presents for a nutrition evaluation. She was admitted after a motor
vehicle collision and was diagnosed with a right tibial plateau fracture, left patellar
fracture, and comminuted coccyx fracture. She has a history of anxiety, diabetes, C
kidney stone, left leg infection, and MRS with debridements. The patient was
instructed to continue to encourage intake > 50 % CCD2, NCS Care and will be
monitored.
